Because the table holds only four entries, classic MBR disks are limited to four primary partitions, a limit GPT removes.
mbtiles: MBTiles is an open specification from Mapbox and can hold either raster image tiles or vector tiles.
It stores tiles in standard SQLite tables keyed by zoom level, column and row, so any SQLite tool can read it.
mbx: MBX is a variant of the Unix mbox format and was the mailbox format Outlook Express used before version 6.
Eudora versions 7 and earlier kept each mail folder in an MBX file while storing attachments separately.
mca: Each MCA region holds a 32x32 grid of chunks - 1,024 in all - prefixed by tables of their offsets and last-saved timestamps.
Chunk data inside is stored in Minecraft’s NBT (Named Binary Tag) tree format, compressed with GZip or Zlib.
mcr: MCR was Minecraft Java Edition’s region format from Beta 1.3 onward, before the Anvil update renamed the files to .mca.
The move to Anvil raised the world’s build height from 128 to 256 blocks by splitting chunks into stacked sections.
mctemplate: A world template carries a manifest that marks it reusable, so importing it spawns a fresh copy rather than one shared world.
Minecraft: Bedrock keeps templates in a separate list from imported worlds, ready to seed new games from a prepared starting point.
mcworld: Opening an MCWORLD on a device running Minecraft: Bedrock imports the world straight into the player’s saves.
Bedrock packages worlds as MCWORLD but assets and add-ons as MCPACK, keeping level data and cosmetics in separate file types.
md2: Quake II’s MD2 stores each vertex as single bytes to save space, then linearly interpolates between keyframes for smooth motion.
To save more room the format indexes a fixed table of 256 precomputed surface normals rather than storing them in full.
md3: Quake III split a character into three MD3 files - legs, torso and head - joined at runtime by massless markers called tags.
Those tags also pin weapons and other props to a model, lining each piece up frame by frame.
mdb: MDB databases are driven by Microsoft’s Jet engine and are capped at two gigabytes in size.
Jet, the engine behind .mdb files, is short for Joint Engine Technology, and it also powered data access in Visual Basic.
mdf: An MDF holds the raw sector data of a copied disc, which is why it captures more than a plain ISO can.
MDF images are produced by Alcohol Soft’s Alcohol 120%, which can mirror discs carrying schemes like SecuROM and SafeDisc.
mdl: The original Quake MDL animated characters by morphing vertices, much like its successor formats MD2 and MD3.
Valve’s GoldSrc engine reworked MDL for Half-Life, replacing vertex morphing with skeletal animation.
mds: An MDS holds no disc contents itself - only metadata such as track layout, sessions and dual-layer break points.
Because it records sub-channel and copy-protection details, an MDS/MDF pair can describe discs an ISO image cannot.
med: MED, short for “Music EDitor”, was written for the Amiga by Finnish programmer Teijo Kinnunen and first released in 1989.
Later OctaMED songs are saved as MMD (“MED MoDule”) files, while the plain MED, MED2, MED3 and MED4 variants trace earlier versions of the editor.
mf: The manifest sits beside the .ovf descriptor in an Open Virtualization Format package, sharing its base name and listing every file’s SHA digest.
Open Virtualization Format is governed by the DMTF and was later published as an ISO standard, with the .mf manifest one of its defined parts.
mht: The format grew out of email standards, encapsulating a web page and its resources the same way a message carries multiple attachments.
Internet Explorer 5 was the first browser to save and open these single-file web archives, popularising “Save as Web Archive”.
mhtml: MHTML stands for “MIME Encapsulation of Aggregate Documents, such as HTML”, the title of the RFC 2557 standard that defines it.
Inside, the page is the HTML part and each image, stylesheet or script follows as a further part keyed to its original web address.
mif: A MIF file rarely travels alone - its companion .mid file holds the attribute data in comma-separated rows, one per object in the same order.
MapInfo Interchange Format was created so MapInfo data could be exported as readable text and re-imported into other geographic information systems.
mlp: MLP was developed by the British hi-fi maker Meridian Audio and is also known as Packed PCM, typically squeezing music by about a third.
Dolby licensed Meridian’s technology to build Dolby TrueHD, the lossless soundtrack format used on Blu-ray discs.
mmcif: mmCIF extends the Crystallographic Information File, the broader IUCr standard for sharing crystal-structure data, to handle huge biological molecules.
Since 2019 the Protein Data Bank has required new crystallographic depositions in PDBx/mmCIF, cementing its place over the legacy PDB layout.
mmf: SMAF was devised by Yamaha and, unlike a plain MIDI tune, can bundle its own instrument samples, PCM sound and even graphics inside one file.
Playback relied on Yamaha’s MA-series sound chips fitted to feature phones from makers like Samsung, Motorola and Panasonic.
mng: MNG was shepherded to its version 1.0 specification by Glenn Randers-Pehrson, the same developer central to the PNG project.
A single MNG can stitch together embedded PNG frames and even JPEG-based JNG images for animations, slide shows or complex stills.
mobi: The format descends from PalmDOC, the document format of Palm OS handhelds, which is why MOBI books can also carry the .prc extension.
Amazon’s early Kindle AZW files were essentially Mobipocket books using its high-compression option, the two being near-identical without DRM.
mobileconfig: A configuration profile is a property-list document holding an array of payloads, each one setting up a separate subsystem such as Wi-Fi, VPN or email.
Profiles can be cryptographically signed and encrypted using the Cryptographic Message Syntax, so settings arrive tamper-proof and private.
mobileprovision: The file is a property list wrapped in a Cryptographic Message Syntax signature, which is why it cannot simply be opened as readable text.
Development profiles list the exact device identifiers allowed to run the app, whereas App Store distribution profiles carry no such device list.
mod: The original format was created by German developer Karsten Obarski for Ultimate Soundtracker, the music tracker he sold for the Amiga in 1987.
Early MOD files were capped at four channels and fifteen samples, matching the Amiga’s “Paula” sound chip that mixed them in hardware.
model: CATIA is Dassault Systèmes’ computer-aided design suite, and the .model file is the part format from its version 4 generation.
Version 5 replaced it with separate .CATPart and .CATProduct files, though V5 can still read the older .model data.
mogrt: Authors build a template in the Essential Graphics panel of After Effects, exposing only chosen controls for editors to tweak in Premiere Pro.
Everything the design needs - source footage, images and pre-compositions - is packaged inside so the template stays self-contained.
mol: The Molfile was introduced by MDL Information Systems in 1979 and centres on a connection table describing a molecule’s atoms and bonds.
Its widely used V2000 layout caps a structure at 999 atoms, so larger molecules need the extended V3000 variant.
mol2: MOL2 organises a molecule into labelled record blocks such as @<TRIPOS>ATOM and @<TRIPOS>BOND, which is how parsers locate each section.
Its atom types encode chemical detail like hybridisation and aromaticity, making MOL2 a richer description than a bare list of coordinates.
mot: Some tools call this the EXORciser format, after the 1975 Motorola development system that popularised it, and the same data also travels as .srec, .s19 or .sx.
Each line begins with an “S” and a digit: an S0 header, S1 to S3 data records and an S7 to S9 terminator end the file.
mp1: Layer I underpinned the Precision Adaptive Sub-band Coding used by Philips’ Digital Compact Cassette, fixed there at 384 kilobits per second.
It is the computationally simplest of the three MPEG-1 audio layers, trading higher bitrates for an easier encoder and decoder.
mp2: MP2 grew out of MUSICAM, a codec developed for the pan-European Eureka 147 project that became the basis of Digital Audio Broadcasting.
Because its frames are independent, a dropout damages only a small slice of sound, which is why broadcasters still favour it.
mpc: Musepack was formerly known as MPEGplus, MP+ or MPC, and its codec is derived from the MPEG-1 Layer II algorithms.
Development began in 1997 with Andree Buschmann, and the codec is open source and deliberately patent-free.
mpd: An MPD is an XML manifest describing periods, adaptation sets and representations, letting a player pick the best quality for current bandwidth.
MPEG-DASH is standardised as ISO/IEC 23009-1 and, unlike many rivals, is deliberately codec-agnostic.
mpk: MessagePack uses a type-length-value scheme where the first byte names the data type and can even hold small integers outright.
It supports the same building blocks as JSON - maps, arrays, strings, numbers, booleans and null - plus a native binary type.
mpkg: A meta-package is an installer that bundles several individual .pkg packages, letting the user pick which components to install.
Inside it a Distribution.dist XML document lists the sub-packages and any scripts the macOS Installer should run.
mpq: “Mo’PaQ” stands for Mike O’Brien Pack, named after the Blizzard programmer who designed it for Diablo.
Files inside are found by hashing their name into an encrypted hash table, and Blizzard later moved on to its CASC system.
msf: The Mail Summary File is a Mork database, a quirky near-text format named after the character Mork from the sitcom “Mork & Mindy”.
Mork was invented at Netscape by David McCusker, and Thunderbird still leans on it for both folder summaries and address books.
msg: An MSG file is a COM structured-storage compound document - the same OLE container Word and Excel use - holding the item’s MAPI properties as streams.
msgpack: MessagePack was created by Sadayuki Furuhashi in 2008 and first saw wide use in his Fluentd logging tool.
It was later built into the Redis data store, helping spread the format across high-performance systems.
msh: Gmsh is a free finite-element mesh generator, and the .msh file records the nodes and elements its mesher produces.
The format carries the geometry split into elements together with the physical groups a solver needs to apply boundary conditions.
msi: An MSI package is really a small relational database stored in Microsoft’s COM structured-storage format, its many tables linked by keys.
Those tables describe every change to the target system, and the structured-storage container even lets an install span multiple discs.
msix: Every MSIX package must be digitally signed, and a block-map file lists cryptographic hashes for each block so it can be verified and updated incrementally.
msu: An MSU bundles the update’s payload as a CAB cabinet alongside an XML manifest of metadata, prerequisites and applicability rules.
The format arrived with Windows Vista and is handled by WUSA, the Windows Update Standalone Installer.
mtm: MultiTracker, released by the demo group Renaissance in 1993, was the first 32-channel tracker on any platform.
Its MTM module is a close sibling of the Amiga MOD format and added support for the Gravis Ultrasound sound card.
mxf: MXF is an SMPTE standard that wraps the actual media - the “essence” - together with descriptive metadata, while staying agnostic to the codec used.
Its many Operational Patterns govern file complexity, with OP1A the workhorse for broadcast video interchange.
n64: Big-endian dumps use the .z64 extension, named after the Z64 backup unit by Interesting Devices, the order the console reads natively.
The .n64 extension flags a little-endian ordering, the least common of the three byte arrangements Nintendo 64 dumps appear in.
nbt: Minecraft creator Markus “Notch” Persson wrote the brief NBT specification, and the files are normally compressed with GZip.
Every value carries a one-byte tag identifying its type, from simple bytes and strings up to nested lists and compound trees.
nc: Numerical control traces back to the MIT Servomechanisms Laboratory in the 1950s, where the first such programming was developed.
APT, the Automatically Programmed Tool language developed at MIT in the late 1950s, became the first widely used way of writing programs for NC machines.
nc4: NetCDF-4 stores its data using the HDF5 file format, which is what lets it hold larger variables, groups and per-variable compression.
NetCDF is developed by the Unidata program at the University Corporation for Atmospheric Research for sharing array-oriented scientific data.
ndjson: The very same format is also called JSON Lines or JSONL, each line being one independent JSON value with no commas between them.
Reading it line by line lets a program stream a multi-gigabyte file without loading the whole thing into memory at once.
nds: An NDS ROM carries two separate ARM programs - ARM9 code for the main processor and ARM7 code for the sound and wireless sub-processor.
The cartridge header packs a four-character game code, a maker code and a Nitro filesystem that indexes the ROM’s bundled assets.
nes: The de-facto NES ROM layout is the iNES format devised by emulator author Marat Fayzullin, which prefixes the program and graphics data with a 16-byte header.
That header’s mapper number tells the emulator which cartridge memory-banking chip the game relied on, a scheme so useful it became the scene standard.
net: SPICE, the simulator that reads these netlists, was written by Laurence Nagel at the University of California, Berkeley in the early 1970s.
A netlist is purely topological text - it names parts and the nodes they share - so the same file can drive simulation or a circuit-board layout.
nfo: The .nfo extension was coined by “Fabulous Furlough” of the warez group The Humble Guys, who used it in place of the usual README file.
The elaborate logos rely on the box-drawing and block glyphs of IBM PC code page 437, so they only render correctly in that DOS character set.
ngc: The “NGC” stands for Next Generation Controller, the project whose RS274NGC dialect of G-code LinuxCNC interprets.
LinuxCNC began life as NIST’s Enhanced Machine Controller (EMC), was renamed EMC2, then became LinuxCNC around 2011 to dodge a trademark clash with EMC Corporation.
nii: NIfTI was devised by a US National Institutes of Health working group as a cleaner successor to the older ANALYZE 7.5 format.
Unlike ANALYZE, which split a scan across separate header and image files, a single .nii file holds both the header and the voxel data together.
nksc: An NKSC sidecar is really an XMP XML file carrying Nikon-specific tags for white balance, exposure and lens corrections.
The edits are non-destructive and live in an “NKSC_PARAM” folder beside the raw, leaving the original NEF untouched.
nmea: The NMEA 0183 standard comes from the National Marine Electronics Association and was built for a 4800-baud serial link between boat instruments.
Each sentence opens with a “$”, a talker and message code, then ends with an optional “*” checksum that is the XOR of every character in between.
npy: A short header at the top of the file stores the array’s data type, shape and whether it is laid out in C or Fortran order, as a Python dictionary literal.
If the array holds Python objects rather than numbers, the data section falls back to a pickle stream instead of a raw memory dump.
npz: Each array inside is stored as its own .npy member, so one NPZ can mix different shapes and data types in a single download.
Saving with NumPy’s savez_compressed applies deflate compression, trading speed for a smaller file than the uncompressed savez.
nrg: Nero Burning ROM was made by the German firm Ahead Software, later renamed Nero AG.
Unlike a plain ISO, an NRG can capture audio CDs and multi-session discs, storing the data in a chain of IFF-style chunks.
nsf: NSF stands for Notes Storage Facility, and it bundles documents, views, forms and design elements together as one self-contained database.
Lotus Notes was built by Ray Ozzie’s Iris Associates before passing to IBM and later HCL.
nsfe: NSFE was created by the developer known as Disch and popularised by the NotSoFatso player to carry richer metadata than plain NSF.
It is built from named chunks holding things like track titles, fade times and playlists, and is not backward-compatible with the original NSF layout.
nsp: An NSP is a PFS0 archive that packs together the encrypted Nintendo Content Archives (NCA) making up a Switch title.
It bundles the program code, game data and a metadata record (CNMT) that lists the title ID, version and required firmware.
numbers: A .numbers file is really a macOS bundle - a folder of internal pieces that Finder presents as a single document.
With iWork ’13 Apple rebuilt the format around Snappy-compressed Protocol Buffer messages, replacing the earlier XML-based contents.
nupkg: A NuGet package is an Open Packaging Conventions container whose root holds a .nuspec XML manifest listing the package id, version, authors and dependencies.
nut: NUT was created by FFmpeg and MPlayer developers who found AVI, Ogg and Matroska too limited, aiming for a compact, patent-free and error-resistant container.
Its design is deliberately Ogg-like but fixes shortcomings such as Matroska’s single-timebase limit, allowing streams with independent timebases.
nvram: Beyond boot order, the file can preserve a virtual machine’s serial-port settings and its UEFI Secure Boot keys between sessions.
It mirrors the battery-backed NVRAM chip of a physical PC, the small store that keeps firmware settings when the power is off.
nwc: An NWC is exported from CAD or laser-scan software, capturing just the geometry and properties Navisworks needs so it can reopen heavy models quickly.
Navisworks was created by a Sheffield firm, a subsidiary of Lightwork Design, before Autodesk bought it in 2007.
nwd: An NWD is self-contained - it bakes in all geometry, object properties and clash tests, making it the format you hand out for review.
Navisworks originated with a Sheffield-based company under Lightwork Design and was acquired by Autodesk in 2007.
nwf: Because an NWF only links to the source CAD files, reopening it pulls in any changes those originals have had since.
It is the recommended working file for clash detection, since the clash results, viewpoints and markup are saved into the NWF itself.
nxml: The name is short for “NLM XML”, the form PubMed Central transforms submitted articles into for archiving.
It follows the Journal Article Tag Suite (JATS), standardised as NISO Z39.96 and descended from the NLM Archiving DTD.
o: Object files are relocatable rather than directly runnable, holding code and data plus a symbol table and relocation entries for the linker to resolve.
Common object-file containers include ELF on Linux and Unix, Mach-O on macOS and COFF-derived formats on Windows.
o5c: An O5C is the change-file twin of O5M, distinguished by an “o5c2” header marker that flags it as a set of edits rather than a full dataset.
Both formats were invented for the osmconvert, osmfilter and osmupdate command-line tools.
o5m: O5M mirrors the structure of plain OSM XML but stores everything as binary numbers, making it far more compact.
It borrows delta coding from the PBF format, recording each id as the small difference from the previous one rather than the full value.
obu: AV1 was created by the Alliance for Open Media, a consortium founded in 2015, as a royalty-free successor to Google’s VP9 codec.
An AV1 stream is packetised into Open Bitstream Units, each tagged with a type, which is what the OBU file extension refers to.
odg: OpenDocument grew out of the XML format created for OpenOffice.org and is overseen by the OASIS standards consortium.
odp: In 2014 the UK government selected OpenDocument as its official standard for sharing editable documents across departments.
ods: OpenDocument was developed under the OASIS consortium and adopted as the ISO/IEC 26300 international standard.
odt: OpenDocument descends from the XML format OpenOffice.org introduced and is standardised internationally as ISO/IEC 26300.
ofr: OptimFROG was written by Florin Ghido and consistently ranks among the highest-compressing lossless audio codecs, accepting slower speed for smaller files.
It even offers a DualStream mode that splits audio into a lossy part plus a correction file that restores full losslessness.
oft: An OFT is built on the same OLE compound-file structure as a saved Outlook message; only its internal storage class marks it as a template.
Opening one spawns a fresh pre-filled email rather than editing the template itself, so the original can be reused indefinitely.
ogm: OGM was created by Tobias Waldvogel as a hack of Xiph’s Ogg container, adding video, multiple audio tracks, subtitles and chapters that AVI handled poorly.
It was always seen as a stopgap and faded once Matroska matured, offering the same features with less overhead.
okt: Oktalyzer takes its name from supporting eight channels, achieved by mixing them in software down to the Amiga’s four hardware channels in real time.
It was written by Armin Sander and sold commercially in the late 1980s, with its final version arriving in 1991.
olm: OLM is the archive format Outlook for Mac introduced with its 2011 release to store mail, contacts, calendars and tasks locally.
It is the Mac counterpart to Windows Outlook’s PST and cannot be opened directly by Outlook on Windows.
opml: OPML was created by Dave Winer at UserLand Software, first serving as the native file format of his Radio UserLand outliner.
Although designed as a generic outline format, it found its lasting use exporting and sharing podcast and RSS subscription lists.
opvault: OPVault became 1Password’s default vault format in 2015, replacing the older Agile Keychain which left item titles and web addresses in plain text.
It was designed by AgileBits and encrypts its contents with AES-256 in GCM mode.
orc: ORC was announced by Hortonworks together with Facebook to overcome the limits of the earlier RCFile and speed up Apache Hive queries.
Unlike RCFile, which treated each column as an opaque blob, ORC retains the table’s type information and writes one file per task.
org: Org mode was created by Carsten Dominik to organise his own notes and tasks, and ships built into GNU Emacs.
Despite being plain text, it doubles as an agenda, a spreadsheet and a literate-programming notebook that can export to HTML, LaTeX and PDF.
osm: OpenStreetMap was started by Steve Coast after the UK’s Ordnance Survey declined to release its mapping data under a free licence.
Map data is published under the Open Database Licence, and the entire planet is offered as either this XML or the more compact binary PBF form.
ost: OST stands for Offline Storage Table, the offline twin of Outlook’s PST mailbox file.
Modern Unicode OST files default to a 50 GB ceiling, up from the legacy ANSI format’s 2 GB limit.
otg: OTG is the OpenDocument drawing template defined by OASIS and standardised as ISO/IEC 26300, built from ZIP-compressed XML.
ott: OTT is the OpenDocument text template from the OASIS standard, the open-format counterpart to a Word .dotx template.
ova: An OVA is simply a TAR archive containing the OVF descriptor, the virtual disk images and a manifest of checksums.
Unpacking an OVA yields the loose OVF files, which together describe the same appliance in editable form.
ovf: OVF was proposed to the DMTF in 2007 by VMware, Dell, HP, IBM, Microsoft and XenSource before becoming a standard.
An OVF package centres on an XML descriptor that lists the machine’s hardware, disks and settings in a vendor-neutral way.
ovpn: An OVPN file can embed the CA, client certificate and private key inline, wrapped in tags so one file is enough to connect.
OpenVPN secures its tunnels with the OpenSSL library, giving the config its certificate-and-key based authentication.
oxps: OXPS is the Ecma-388 standard form of XPS, approved by Ecma International in 2009.
Windows 8 switched its document writer to OXPS, replacing the older XPS as the default output.
p12: PKCS#12 is one of the Public-Key Cryptography Standards published by RSA Laboratories.
The format grew out of Microsoft’s PFX, which RSA formalised as PKCS#12 while keeping binary compatibility.
p65: PageMaker 6.5 introduced layers and text frames, modernising the program Adobe had inherited when it bought Aldus.
PageMaker helped launch the desktop-publishing revolution before Adobe phased it out in favour of InDesign.
p7b: P7B follows the PKCS#7 Cryptographic Message Syntax, a standard from RSA Laboratories.
Windows and Java Tomcat are the platforms that most commonly hand out certificate chains as P7B files.
p7c: A P7C is a degenerate PKCS#7 SignedData structure - a “certs-only” bundle with no actual signed content.
P7C uses the same PKCS#7 syntax as S/MIME, which is why mail tools recognise it as a certificate carrier.
p7m: P7M is built on the PKCS#7 / CMS syntax, the same cryptographic message format used across S/MIME.
A P7M can hold either enveloped (encrypted) or signed-and-enclosed mail, mail clients showing it as smime.p7m.
p7s: A P7S holds a detached PKCS#7 SignedData object, kept separate from the message it vouches for.
Mail clients attach the signature as smime.p7s inside a multipart/signed S/MIME message.
p8: Apple’s push-notification P8 keys hold an elliptic-curve key in a PKCS#8 wrapper, stored without a password.
Apps use a P8 key to sign the JSON Web Tokens that authenticate them to Apple Push Notification service.
pab: The Personal Address Book was a MAPI component separate from Outlook’s own Contacts folder.
Microsoft deprecated the PAB from Outlook 2002, and Outlook 2010 can only import one, never use it live.
package: Sims .package files use Maxis’ DBPF (DataBase Packed File) container, identifiable by its “DBPF” header.
The same DBPF container holds data across many Maxis games, from SimCity 4 to Spore and The Sims 4.
pages: Recent Pages files store their content as IWA archives - Google’s Protocol Buffers compressed with Snappy.
Older iWork ’09 Pages documents instead kept their content in a plain index.xml inside the bundle.
pak: A Quake PAK opens with the magic letters “PACK” and is just an uncompressed table of files and paths.
From Quake III onward id Software dropped PAK for ZIP-based .pk3 archives, though unrelated engines still reuse the .pak name.
pam: PAM stands for Portable Arbitrary Map and carries the Netpbm magic number P7.
PAM was added to the Netpbm family in 2000 to bring an alpha channel and arbitrary tuples to the simple maps.
par: Solid Edge was first built by Intergraph in 1996 before passing to UGS and then to Siemens.
Solid Edge models its PAR parts on the Parasolid kernel, adopted after the move away from the original ACIS kernel.
parquet: Parquet was created in 2013 by engineers at Twitter and Cloudera, drawing on Google’s Dremel paper.
Its record-shredding columnar layout lets queries read just the columns they need, skipping the rest of the file.
part: Firefox writes a download to a .part file first, renaming it to the real name only once the transfer finishes.
Because a .part is an incomplete copy, it is normally unusable until the download completes.
pat: A single PAT file can hold a whole library of tileable patterns for filling and texturing in Photoshop.
patch: The unified diff in a patch marks each block of changes with an “@@” hunk header giving the affected line ranges.
Patch files prefix added lines with “+” and removed lines with “-”, keeping surrounding context for safe application.
pb: Protocol Buffers were open-sourced by Google in 2008 as a smaller, faster alternative to text formats like JSON.
A .pb stream is not self-describing - it needs its .proto schema to be decoded, and pairs naturally with gRPC.
pbf: PBF is built on Google’s Protocol Buffers, packing map data far more tightly than the equivalent XML.
OpenStreetMap planet dumps and Mapbox vector tiles both use .pbf yet follow completely different, unrelated schemas.
pbm: Jef Poskanzer invented PBM in the 1980s so images could survive being sent through 7-bit-only email.
PBM stores one bit per pixel, the most minimal member of the Netpbm family.
pcap: The pcap format came out of the libpcap and tcpdump work at Lawrence Berkeley Laboratory in the late 1980s.
A pcap begins with a magic number that also signals the file’s byte order to whatever reads it.
pcapng: Every pcapng capture opens with a Section Header Block, the marker that distinguishes it from classic pcap.
Pcapng became Wireshark’s default save format in 2012 with version 1.8.
pcd: PCD was created by Radu B. Rusu and can store points as plain ASCII or as a compact binary dump.
Version 0.7 of the format added a VIEWPOINT header recording the sensor’s position and orientation.
pce: The PC Engine launched in Japan in 1987, built by Hudson Soft and manufactured by NEC.
PC Engine games shipped on credit-card-sized HuCard ROM cards that slotted into the front of the console.
pck: A Godot PCK is identified by its “GDPC” header and stores an MD5 checksum for each packed file.
Developers can embed the PCK inside the game executable, or ship it separately as a downloadable patch or mod.
pcx: PCX stands for PiCture eXchange and was the native format of ZSoft’s PC Paintbrush program.
PCX squeezes its pixels with simple run-length encoding, collapsing repeated runs into compact pairs.
pdn: Paint.NET is written in C# on Microsoft’s .NET platform, and a PDN preserves its layers and transparency.
The PDN file is a compressed dump of the editor’s internal document object rather than a generic image format.
pem: A PEM file is base64-encoded DER wrapped between BEGIN and END lines that label what it contains.
Because PEM only defines the wrapper, one file can hold almost anything - certificates, keys or chains.
pgp: Phil Zimmermann named PGP after “Ralph’s Pretty Good Grocery” from Garrison Keillor’s fictional Lake Wobegon.
Exporting PGP made Zimmermann the target of a US arms-export investigation that was dropped without charges in 1996.
pgw: PGW follows ESRI’s world-file naming trick, taking a PNG’s first and last letters plus a “w”.
The six numbers in a world file set scale, rotation and origin, but say nothing about which map projection applies.
pickle: A pickle is really a little program for a stack-based virtual machine that rebuilds the object opcode by opcode.
Pickle has several protocol versions, the oldest being a human-readable ASCII form for backward compatibility.
pk3: The “PK” stands for “package”, and PK3 replaced the earlier Quake PAK format used by the first two Quake games.
The id Tech 4 engine behind Doom 3 carried the idea forward with the near-identical PK4, again just a renamed ZIP.
pkg: A modern flat PKG is an Apple-flavoured XAR archive whose payload is a gzip-compressed cpio bundle of the files to be installed.
Each component carries a “bill of materials” (Bom) and an XML PackageInfo describing exactly what will be written to the disc.
pkl: Converting a Python object to a pickle byte stream is called “pickling”, and reading it back is “unpickling”.
There are six pickle protocol versions; protocol 5, added in Python 3.8, supports out-of-band buffers for very large data.
plist: Apple deprecated the original NeXTSTEP plist syntax in Mac OS X 10.0, replacing it with XML, then added a compact binary form in 10.2.
Binary property lists are split into a header, an object table, an offset table and an 8-byte trailer that indexes the whole file.
pls: PLS was created by Nullsoft for the Winamp media player and uses an INI-style layout with a [playlist] header.
Each entry can carry a title and a track length in seconds, with -1 marking an unknown length such as a live stream.
pm6: PageMaker began as Aldus PageMaker in 1985 and only became an Adobe product after Adobe bought Aldus in 1994.
Adobe eventually retired PageMaker in favour of InDesign, which shipped in 1999 as its intended successor.
pmd: PageMaker was written by the Seattle start-up Aldus, founded by Paul Brainerd, and is credited with coining the term “desktop publishing”.
It leaned on Adobe’s PostScript and the Apple LaserWriter to put professional typesetting on ordinary desks.
pmtiles: PMTiles was created by Brandon Liu of Protomaps as a cloud-optimised, serverless alternative to the SQLite-based MBTiles format.
A reader can fetch any tile from a PMTiles archive over plain HTTP range requests, needing at most two requests whatever the file’s size.
pnm: The PNM family was invented by Jef Poskanzer in the 1980s so that images could survive being mailed as plain 7-bit ASCII text.
Each member starts with a two-byte “magic” of P followed by a digit that selects bitmap, greymap or pixmap in ASCII or binary form.
pol: Windows stores one Registry.pol for computer settings and another for user settings under the GroupPolicy folder.
Inside, registry instructions are written as bracketed, semicolon-separated fields in UTF-16 little-endian text.
poscar: POSCAR lists the lattice vectors, a scaling constant and the position of every atom, in either Cartesian or fractional coordinates.
The cell is always treated as periodic in three dimensions, so a surface or molecule is modelled by adding a wide vacuum gap.
pp3: “PP3” is short for Processing Profile version 3, the plain-text recipe of every adjustment RawTherapee makes to a photo.
Because the edits live in the sidecar, the original raw file is never altered and a recipe can be copied to other shots.
ppf: PPF was created by Icarus of the group Paradox specifically to patch CD-based console images, which the older IPS format could not handle.
The format went through three revisions, PPF1 to PPF3, applied with tools such as PPF-O-Matic.
ppk: PPK is PuTTY’s own key format; version 3, introduced in 2021, added Argon2 key-stretching and an SHA-256 MAC over the weaker SHA-1 of version 2.
Keys are created and converted by PuTTYgen, PuTTY’s companion key-generation utility.
ppm: PPM stores 24-bit colour as eight bits each of red, green and blue, with no compression at all.
It is the colour member of Jef Poskanzer’s Netpbm trio, sitting alongside the black-and-white PBM and greyscale PGM.
ppsx: Double-clicking a PPSX launches the slideshow directly with no PowerPoint editing interface visible.
ppt: PowerPoint was created at Forethought, not Microsoft, and launched in 1987 for the Macintosh before Microsoft bought the company.
From PowerPoint 97 the .ppt format was built on Microsoft’s Compound File Binary structure, the same OLE container used by Word and Excel.
prel: A PREL project is written in XML and uses the same internal format as professional Premiere Pro’s PRPROJ files.
Like Premiere Pro, later versions wrap that XML in gzip compression to keep project files small.
prj: A .prj is a sidecar for Esri’s shapefile, which deliberately splits a dataset across several files each with its own role.
Its single line of Well-Known Text names the datum, ellipsoid, projection method and units of the data’s coordinate system.
procreate: Procreate is made by Savage Interactive, a studio based in Hobart, Tasmania, Australia.
Inside the file, each layer is stored as LZO-compressed RGBA tiles together with a thumbnail and an optional time-lapse recording.
properties: Before Java 9 a .properties file had to be encoded in ISO-8859-1, with any other character written as a \uHHHH escape.
Grouped into ResourceBundles, these files are Java’s standard way of translating an application into different languages.
proto: A .proto file is compiled by the protoc compiler, which generates data-access classes for many programming languages.
The same schema can serialise to a compact binary wire format or, alternatively, to JSON and a human-readable text form.
prproj: Up to Creative Suite 6 a PRPROJ was plain XML; from Premiere Pro CC onward Adobe wraps that XML in gzip compression.
Unzipped, the file reveals readable text for every sequence setting, bin, clip and effect on the timeline.
prt: The PRT made by Siemens NX (formerly Unigraphics) is built on the Parasolid geometry kernel, while PTC’s PRT comes from Pro/ENGINEER and Creo.
Because each program writes its own proprietary structure, an NX PRT and a Creo PRT are not interchangeable despite the shared extension.
ps: PostScript is a full stack-based, Turing-complete programming language, not merely a static description of a page.
It was created by Adobe co-founders John Warnock and Chuck Geschke, and its later offshoot the Camelot project became PDF.
psb: PSB stands for “Photoshop Big”, and it can store canvases up to 300,000 by 300,000 pixels and files of around four exabytes.
psd: Photoshop began as a program called Display, written by Thomas Knoll on a Macintosh Plus and developed with his brother John of Industrial Light & Magic.
Before Adobe took over, early copies shipped bundled with Barneyscan slide scanners; Adobe bought the rights outright in 1995.
psf: PSF was publicly documented by Neill Corlett, who also wrote the “Highly Experimental” Winamp plug-in to play the files.
“PSF” first meant PlayStation Sound Format, and the generic “Portable” backronym arrived once Saturn, Dreamcast, N64 and other variants were added.
psm: PSM is the sheet-metal part format of Solid Edge, the mechanical CAD package now developed by Siemens.
pst: PST stands for Personal Storage Table, the on-disc file Outlook uses to hold mail, contacts and calendar items.
The old ANSI variant was capped at 2 GB, whereas the modern Unicode format supports far larger mailboxes.
pt6: Aldus, the company that created PageMaker, took its name from Aldus Manutius, the 15th-century Venetian printer who pioneered the portable book.
Templates like this fed PageMaker, the program widely credited with launching the desktop-publishing era.
pts: PTS is a plain ASCII format whose first line is the point count, followed by X, Y, Z, intensity and optional R, G, B per line.
It originates with Leica Geosystems and is the “dumb” cousin of PTX, which adds each scan’s position and orientation matrix.
ptx: PTX arrived with Pro Tools 10, replacing the earlier PTF and PTS session formats.
Pro Tools is made by Avid, which gained the software when it acquired Digidesign in 1995.
pub: A .pub key is one half of an asymmetric key pair, safe to copy onto any server while its matching private key stays secret.
On an SSH server the public key is appended to the user’s authorized_keys file to permit password-free logins.
pvk: A PVK pairs with a Software Publisher Certificate (.spc) and the two are usually merged into a .pfx file with Microsoft’s pvk2pfx tool.
It holds the private half of an Authenticode key used to sign Windows executables, drivers and installers.
pvm: A PVM is actually a macOS package bundle, gathering the VM’s virtual hard disc, logs and disk images in one folder.
Inside it a config.pvs file stores the machine’s settings - memory, network and hardware - as XML.
pwd: PWD is the weldment format of Solid Edge, the mechanical CAD system now owned by Siemens.
It captures an assembly of parts joined by welds, letting the design carry weld call-outs and related data.
pxn: PXN is the raw format of the Logitech Fotoman, one of the first commercially sold digital cameras, launched in the early 1990s.
Its images were just 8-bit greyscale at 376 by 240 pixels, so genuine PXN files are quite rare today.
pyc: A .pyc begins with a magic number that changes whenever Python’s bytecode does, so files from one version are rejected by another.
Since Python 3.2 these caches live in a per-package __pycache__ folder, tagged with the interpreter name and version.
pyxel: Pyxel Edit specialises in tilesets and can scan an imported image to detect and de-duplicate its unique tiles automatically.
Finished work can be exported as a sprite sheet or animated GIF, or as a tilemap in XML, JSON or plain text.
qb: The format was made by Minddesk for its Qubicle editor and stores several separate voxel matrices in one file, handy for models built from many parts.
QB optionally compresses its voxel data with run-length encoding and can pack a face-visibility mask into the alpha byte for faster rendering.
qcow2: QCOW2 supports two compression schemes for stored data - zlib (deflate) and the newer Zstandard - though compressed clusters are read-only.
A QCOW2 image can layer changes on top of a read-only backing file, recording only the differences so the base image is never modified.
qcp: QCP is a container based on Microsoft’s RIFF structure and typically wraps speech encoded with Qualcomm’s QCELP or EVRC codecs.
The format was designed to squeeze human voice down for CDMA cellular networks, with QCELP running at either 8 or 13 kilobits per second.
qgs: A QGS file is plain XML describing a map’s layers, symbology and settings rather than the geographic data itself, which stays in separate source files.
QGS was QGIS’s default project format until version 3.2, when the zipped QGZ variant took over as the standard.
qgz: A QGZ bundle holds the project’s QGS xml plus a QGD file, an SQLite database carrying auxiliary data such as embedded styles.
QGZ became QGIS’s default save format in version 3.2, replacing the bare QGS file as the standard project container.
qmd: Quarto is built on the Pandoc converter and is language-agnostic, running R, Python, Julia and Observable JavaScript in the same document.
Quarto was made by Posit, the company formerly known as RStudio, and announced at its 2022 conference alongside that corporate rebrand.
qoi: QOI was invented by Dominic Szablewski, who donated the specification to the public domain under CC0 so anyone can implement it freely.
Its whole specification fits on a single page, and the author claimed encoding 20 to 50 times faster than PNG at similar file sizes.
qxd: QXD is the native document of QuarkXPress, the desktop-publishing program made by Quark, and was superseded by the QXP format in version 6.
QXD files are the work format only of QuarkXPress 5 and earlier, holding a page layout’s text, images and positioning together.
r3d: REDCODE compresses footage with a wavelet transform at a variable bitrate, devoting more data to complex frames to keep raw flexibility while taming file size.
RED’s R3D codec debuted with the RED ONE camera in 2007, bringing raw, non-destructive workflows to digital cinema.
rar: RAR uses a proprietary LZ-based algorithm that is not licensed to third-party compressors, so other tools may decompress RAR files but cannot create them.
ras: Sun Raster was the native bitmap of Sun Microsystems’ SunOS workstations and could store everything from 1-bit mono to 32-bit colour.
The format offers an optional run-length encoding scheme to shrink images, and stores its true-colour pixels as BGR rather than RGB.
rda: An RDA or RData file can hold many R objects at once and, unlike RDS, restores them under their original variable names.
R has used the same version 2 serialisation behind these files since R 1.4.0 in December 2001, with optional gzip, bzip2 or xz compression.
rdata: RData uses R’s portable XDR big-endian encoding so a workspace saved on one platform reloads correctly on any other.
Saving with save.image() captures the whole current workspace, and load() brings every stored object back under its original name.
rds: RDS holds exactly one serialised R object and, unlike an RData workspace, does not record its name, so you can reload it into any variable.
RDS files are written and read with R’s saveRDS() and readRDS() functions and can be compressed with gzip, bzip2 or xz.
rdb: An RDB file is Redis’s point-in-time snapshot of the whole dataset, compact and quick to reload but able to lose writes made since the last save.
Its alternative is the append-only file, which logs every write command instead of snapshotting; modern Redis can combine both.
rec: One common .rec is a Topfield PVR recording, often nicknamed a Toppy file, which is essentially an MPEG transport stream wrapped with a program-info header.
reg: Modern Windows writes .reg files as UTF-16 under the header “Windows Registry Editor Version 5.00”, replacing the older ANSI “REGEDIT4” format.
resource: Unity stores bulky binary assets like audio and video in a .resource file that sits alongside the serialised scene data in a build or asset bundle.
The companion .resS file plays the same role for textures and meshes, keeping large media out of the main serialised file.
rf64: RF64 was specified by the European Broadcasting Union and adds a mandatory ds64 chunk whose 64-bit fields replace WAV’s 32-bit size limits.
A recorder can reserve a placeholder JUNK chunk in a normal broadcast WAV and switch it to RF64 on the fly the moment recording passes 4 GB.
rfa: An RFA is a loadable Revit component such as a window, door or piece of furniture that can be stored in libraries and reused across projects.
Every RFA is built starting from an RFT template, which supplies the new family’s basic structure and parameters.
rft: An RFT is purely a starting point used to author new Revit families and is never placed into a project itself.
It defines the basic structure and parameters a new family inherits, much as a project template seeds a new Revit model.
rm: RealMedia was created by RealNetworks as a container pairing its RealVideo and RealAudio codecs, and it streams content at a constant bitrate.
For locally stored files RealNetworks made a variable-bitrate sibling, RMVB, which adjusts the data rate to scene complexity.
rmd: R Markdown rests on knitr, written by Yihui Xie as a more general replacement for Sweave, and on Pandoc, which renders the output.
Knitr runs the embedded code and converts the document to plain Markdown, which Pandoc then turns into HTML, PDF, Word and more.
rmi: RMI is Microsoft’s wrapper that places a MIDI sequence inside a RIFF container, identified by the RMID chunk tag.
The MIDI Manufacturers Association later extended it so a file could embed a DLS instrument bank, letting the tune carry its own sounds.
rmvb: RMVB is the variable-bitrate sibling of RealNetworks’ RealMedia, allocating more data to complex scenes and less to simple ones.
Unlike the constant-bitrate RM used for streaming, RMVB was aimed at locally stored files and became a staple for sharing Asian films and TV.
romfs: romfs is a minimal read-only filesystem that has shipped in the Linux kernel since version 2.1.21, popular for boot disks and embedded devices.
It stores files sequentially on 16-byte boundaries and protects the start of the image with a checksum, trading features for tiny code size.
rpm: An RPM is built from four parts - a fixed lead, a signature, a metadata header and a payload that is usually a gzip-compressed cpio archive.
RPM was written in 1997 by Erik Troan and Marc Ewing of Red Hat, drawing on earlier packaging experiments named pms, rpp and pm.
rpp: A REAPER project is a human-readable text file using a custom nested block syntax, and it stores only path references to media rather than the audio itself.
Starting a project also spawns companion RPP-BAK backup and RPP-UNDO history files beside the main RPP.
rpyc: An RPYC is the compiled form of a Ren’Py .rpy script, stored as a Python pickle of the engine’s abstract syntax tree.
Visual novels are often shipped with only the RPYC files and the source removed, which obscures the original game script.
rss: RSS began at Netscape in 1999 for its My.Netscape.com portal; Dave Winer’s UserLand later carried the format and shipped RSS 2.0.
The initialism has stood for different things over time, from RDF Site Summary to the now-common Really Simple Syndication of RSS 2.0.
rst: reStructuredText was designed by David Goodger and first released in 2001 as part of the Python Docutils project.
Since 2008 it has been the core markup of Sphinx, the documentation generator behind much of the Python ecosystem.
rte: An RTE seeds a new Revit project with standard settings, views and content, much as RFT does for individual families.
Revit version 1.0 shipped in 2000 from Revit Technology Corporation, the firm Autodesk bought in 2002 and whose templates use the RTE extension.
rtf: RTF was formalised in 1987 by Microsoft Word developers including Charles Simonyi, and first shipped with Word 3.0 for Macintosh.
Its formatting is carried by backslash control words within 7-bit ASCII text; Microsoft froze the specification at version 1.9.1 in 2008.
rtfd: RTFD began on NeXTSTEP in the late 1980s as Rich Text with Attachments, extending RTF so a document could embed images and other files.
Inside the bundle a file named TXT.rtf holds the formatting while attachments sit beside it in their native formats.
rtttl: RTTTL was devised by Nokia and ties into its Smart Messaging system, so monophonic tunes could be sent to handsets over SMS.
A ringtone is written as three colon-separated parts - a name, default note values, and the notes themselves with duration, pitch and octave.
rules: Each rule has a header that sets the action, protocol, addresses, ports and traffic direction, followed by options defining the payload to match.
Suricata was built to understand Snort’s rule syntax, so the same .rules files can often feed both detection engines.
rvdata2: RVDATA2 files are Ruby objects serialised with Marshal.dump by RPG Maker VX Ace’s RGSS3 scripting system, made by Enterbrain.
Because they rely on Ruby’s binary Marshal format, the maps, databases and save states inside are unreadable in an ordinary text editor.
rvt: Revit version 1.0, which writes the RVT format, shipped in 2000 from Revit Technology Corporation, a firm Autodesk acquired in 2002.
RVT supports building information modelling across a structure’s whole lifecycle, from design through construction to maintenance and demolition.
rwz: Rawzor is fully lossless and reversible, so decompressing an RWZ returns the exact original raw file with all its metadata intact.
It typically saves 20 to 60 per cent of space across many camera raws - Canon CR2, Nikon NEF, Sony ARW, Adobe DNG and more - far beating zip or RAR.
rxdata: RXDATA files are Ruby objects written out by Marshal.dump, so opening one without RPG Maker’s Ruby environment is awkward.
Scripts.rxdata is special - it holds the game’s Ruby code as zlib-compressed blobs rather than maps or database tables.
s19: Motorola devised S-records in the mid-1970s for the 6800 processor, encoding binary firmware as printable ASCII hex.
An S19 file uses a 16-bit address field, so it can only span the first 64 KB of memory.
s28: The S28 variant uses a 24-bit address field, extending an S-record’s reach to 16 MB of memory.
PROM programmers of the era read S-record files directly, burning the decoded bytes into PROM and EPROM chips.
s37: The S37 variant carries a 32-bit address field, the widest of the family, covering up to 4 GB.
Address bytes in every S-record are stored big-endian, and each line ends with a checksum byte for integrity.
s3m: S3M succeeded Scream Tracker 2’s simpler STM format, adding up to 32 channels and richer playback effects.
Uniquely for its day, S3M could mix digital samples with AdLib OPL2 FM-synth instruments in the same tune.
safetensors: A safetensors file begins with an 8-byte little-endian length, then a JSON header listing each tensor’s name, shape, dtype and byte offsets.
Because the weight data sits in one contiguous block, loaders can memory-map it straight from disk without copying it through RAM.
sam: Each SAM line is a tab-separated record whose flag field is a bitmask describing how that read mapped to the reference.
BAM is simply SAM’s compressed binary twin, and both were defined by the 1000 Genomes Project’s data-processing group.
sap: SAP stands for Slight Atari Player, the program for which the format was designed.
A SAP file embeds the original 6502 player code, so a player effectively emulates the Atari to produce the sound.
sarif: SARIF began at Microsoft before being handed to OASIS, which approved version 2.1.0 of the JSON schema.
GitHub code scanning ingests SARIF files, turning third-party analysers’ findings into repository alerts.
sas7bdat: SAS Institute never published the SAS7BDAT layout, so open-source readers had to reverse-engineer its paged binary structure.
Internally the file is a header followed by fixed-size pages tagged as metadata, data or a mix of both.
sat: SAT stands for Standard ACIS Text, the human-readable sibling of ACIS’s compact binary SAB format.
Each SAT file opens with a header carrying the ACIS version stamp, then a sequence of entity records describing the solid model.
sav: SPSS originally stood for Statistical Package for the Social Sciences, first released in 1968.
SPSS passed to IBM in 2009, and the product is now officially called IBM SPSS Statistics.
saz: SAZ stands for Session Archive Zip, the native capture format of Eric Lawrence’s Fiddler proxy.
A SAZ may be left open or locked with weak ZIP encryption or stronger 256-bit AES protection.
sbn: Esri never documented the SBN layout, so its binary structure had to be reverse-engineered by the open-source community.
The SBN index is optional - a shapefile parses fine without it, but spatial queries run faster when it is present.
sbs: An SBS is XML, holding the editable node graph, while the published SBSAR is a compiled, read-only archive you cannot turn back into an SBS.
Substance Designer came from Allegorithmic, the French studio Adobe bought in 2019.
sbsar: An SBSAR is the published, self-contained build of an editable SBS graph, embedding everything needed to render the material on its own.
Publishing to SBSAR is one-way - the compiled archive cannot be decompiled back into its source SBS graph.
sbx: SBX is the lightweight companion to the SBN index, pointing into it to speed shape lookups, and both are written only by Esri software.
Like the SBN it pairs with, the SBX format was never documented by Esri and is purely an optional accelerator for a shapefile.
scd: SCD shares the same XML basis as Scribus’s current SLA format, so it can still be opened by modern Scribus.
It comes from very early 0.x releases of Scribus, before SLA became the standard document extension.
sch: EAGLE stands for Easily Applicable Graphical Layout Editor, and its .sch schematic pairs with a .brd board layout.
From version 6 onward EAGLE switched its .sch files from a proprietary binary format to plain XML.
scr: Windows launches it with command-line switches - /s to run, /p to preview and /c to show its settings dialog.
scriv: A .scriv project is really a folder package, with the manuscript stored as many separate RTF files.
A master XML file with a .scrivx extension binds the pieces together and shares the project’s name.
sdb: Shim databases are applied with the built-in sdbinst.exe tool, which registers them so Windows can hook the target program.
Because shims can inject DLLs and redirect calls, attackers have abused the .sdb mechanism for stealthy persistence.
sdc: SDC was the file of StarCalc, the spreadsheet in the StarOffice 5 suite.
Under the bonnet it uses Microsoft’s Compound File container, the same OLE structure as old Office binaries.
sdd: SDD was the file of StarImpress, the presentation tool in the StarOffice 5 suite.
StarOffice traced back to StarWriter in 1985 and was bought by Sun Microsystems in 1999.
sdf: An SDF wraps the MDL Molfile, appending tagged data items after each molecule.
Records inside an SDF are separated by a line of four dollar signs, $$$$.
sdw: SDW was the file of StarWriter, the word processor at the heart of StarOffice 5.
It is built on Microsoft’s Compound File container, the OLE structure also used by legacy Word documents.
segy: SEG-Y was created by the Society of Exploration Geophysicists to record seismic data onto half-inch magnetic tape.
Each file still opens with a 3200-byte EBCDIC text header, a legacy of the 40 punched-card images that once described a survey.
service: Service unit files use INI-style syntax, grouped into [Unit], [Service] and [Install] sections.
systemd was started by Lennart Poettering and Kay Sievers at Red Hat in 2010 to replace the old System V init.
session: The .session format dates from CATIA Version 4, bundling together the .model files a designer was using.
Opening a V4 .session inside the later CATIA V5 requires linking back to the databases that hold its models.
sesx: SESX replaced Audition’s older binary .ses format, switching the session to a readable XML description.
The file holds only edits and pointers to the source audio, so multitrack editing in Audition stays non-destructive.
set: An EEGLAB .set file is actually a MATLAB .mat structure, usually paired with a separate .fdt file holding the raw signal data.
EEGLAB is developed at the Swartz Center for Computational Neuroscience in San Diego.
sf2: SoundFont was created in the early 1990s by E-mu Systems and Creative Labs, and the file is built on the RIFF container.
SoundFont 2.0, released in 1996, was published as an open specification in a bid to make it an industry standard.
sf3: SF3 was devised by MuseScore as a near-identical variant of SF2 whose samples are squeezed with Ogg Vorbis.
The Ogg compression can leave an SF3 roughly ten times smaller than the equivalent uncompressed SF2 bank.
sfz: SFZ was created by Cakewalk in the early 2000s as a plain-text, human-editable way to map sampled instruments.
Being just text, an SFZ holds no audio itself - it points to separate sample files and describes how to play them.
sgi: The SGI image format was invented by Paul Haeberli as the native raster format of Silicon Graphics workstations.
It begins with a 512-byte header and can store pixels either uncompressed or run-length encoded.
sgy: SGY is just an alternative extension for SEG-Y, the seismic format created by the Society of Exploration Geophysicists.
The format still carries its tape-era 3200-byte EBCDIC text header followed by a 400-byte binary header.
shp: A shapefile never stands alone - the .shp geometry needs at least the .shx index and a .dbf attribute table alongside it.
Esri published the shapefile specification openly, which helped it become the de-facto exchange format across rival GIS tools.
shx: SHX records the byte offset and length of every shape in the .shp, letting software jump straight to a feature.
It is one of a shapefile’s mandatory trio - the .shp, .shx and .dbf must all travel together.
sid: MrSID stands for Multi-resolution Seamless Image Database, a wavelet-based format developed and patented by LizardTech.
The technology behind MrSID was first developed at the Los Alamos National Laboratory before LizardTech commercialised it.
sig: A detached signature is verified with the signer’s public key, so any later change to the file makes the check fail.
Such signatures are commonly produced with PGP or GnuPG, and ASCII-armoured ones use the related .asc extension.
simg: A sparse image marks unused regions as “don’t care” chunks, so only meaningful data is written and transferred.
Tools like simg2img expand the sparse file back into a full raw image before it can be mounted or examined.
sit: StuffIt was written in 1987 by Raymond Lau, then a high-school student, and later sold by Aladdin Systems.
Unlike ZIP, StuffIt preserved a Mac file’s resource fork, which was essential to classic Macintosh documents and applications.
sitx: StuffIt X added algorithms such as PPM, BWT and LZMA, plus strong encryption and Unicode file names.
The format was later owned by Smith Micro Software, which declared StuffIt end-of-life in 2019.
skel: The binary .skel export is far smaller and loads faster than Spine’s equivalent JSON skeleton data.
Spine reads the skeleton through its runtimes, which support dozens of game engines and programming languages.
sketch: Sketch is made by the Dutch firm Bohemian Coding, now Sketch B.V., and won an Apple Design Award in 2012.
skp: SketchUp began at the Boulder start-up @Last Software, whose SKP format debuted with the program in 2000.
After Google’s ownership it passed to Trimble in 2012, which still maintains SketchUp and the SKP format today.
sla: Scribus runs on Linux, Windows and macOS alike, from the same source code.
The SLA document is XML inside, recording the Scribus version, page geometry, text and references to placed images.
sldasm: SolidWorks founder Jon Hirschtick bankrolled the company with around a million dollars he had won as a member of the famous MIT Blackjack Team.
An assembly file references its component part files rather than embedding them, so the parts must travel with it.
slddrw: The free eDrawings viewer opens SolidWorks drawings, so suppliers and colleagues without a SolidWorks licence can still review them.
A drawing file links back to the 3D part or assembly it documents, updating its views when that model changes.
sldprt: Under the bonnet a .sldprt is a Microsoft OLE compound document, the same structured-storage container technology used by classic .doc and .xls files.
A part file holds one component’s feature-based history, the recipe of operations that build up its 3D solid.
sln: Despite the name, an SLN is not XML but a custom plain-text layout listing each project by a unique GUID.
Microsoft has begun replacing it with a simpler XML-based SLNX solution format supported by MSBuild.
smc: The .smc copier added a 512-byte header to the dumped cartridge, marked by the bytes 0xAA 0xBB at offsets 8 and 9.
smd: After a 512-byte header, an SMD stores each 16 KB block interleaved as all even bytes then all odd bytes.
De-interleaving tools convert SMD back to a plain sequential binary ROM that most emulators prefer.
sms: The Master System’s Z80 could address only 64 KB, so larger cartridges relied on bank switching to page in ROM.
Related Sega systems reuse the same scheme with their own extensions, such as .gg for the Game Gear.
snap: A snap is a single SquashFS image bundling the app, its libraries and metadata, managed by the snapd daemon.
Snaps run sandboxed, with confinement enforced through Linux kernel features such as AppArmor, seccomp and cgroups.
snb: SNB was made by Shanda’s subsidiary for the Bambook reader and carried DRM to lock e-books to the device.
The format was released in China around 2011 and saw little adoption beyond the Bambook itself.
snd: SND was the native sound format on NeXTSTEP, used for system alerts and the platform’s Sound applications.
Its 24-byte big-endian header can describe μ-law or A-law audio, the same logarithmic codecs used in telephony.
so: The name stands for shared object, the Unix equivalent of a Windows DLL loaded by several programs at once.
Such libraries are ELF files whose embedded soname lets the dynamic linker pick a compatible version at runtime.
sp: Laurence Nagel wrote the original SPICE as a Berkeley graduate student under professor Donald Pederson, who insisted the simulator be given away freely.
The original SPICE was written in FORTRAN and released into the public domain, seeding countless commercial simulators.
spc: The SPC700 was a custom sound CPU that Sony designed for the SNES, driving a separate DSP for audio.
Because the file is a full 64 KB memory dump, even a brief tune occupies the same fixed size.
spi: In 2011 the IEEE declared SPICE an official Milestone of electrical engineering, unveiling a plaque at Berkeley’s Cory Hall where it was written.
A netlist names each component and the numbered nodes it connects, which the simulator turns into equations to solve.
spice: SPICE stands for Simulation Program with Integrated Circuit Emphasis and was first presented by Berkeley in 1973.
It grew out of an earlier program called CANCER, rewritten so it could be placed in the public domain.
spine: Spine animates characters with bones and meshes rather than drawn frames, keeping file size and artwork small.
Projects export to JSON or compact binary data that Spine’s runtimes load across many game engines.
splat: The format was popularised by web viewers and is essentially a compact binary serialisation of Gaussian-splat PLY data.
Each splat records a position, colour, opacity and an oriented shape, blended together to reconstruct a scene.
spp: Substance Painter lets artists paint textures directly onto a 3D model following a physically based rendering workflow.
Since version 8.2 the application stores its own version number inside each saved SPP project file.
spx: Speex uses CELP coding and offers narrowband, wideband and ultra-wideband modes tuned for low-bitrate speech.
The project began in 2002 under Jean-Marc Valin and was formally deprecated in 2013 in favour of Opus.
spz: The name stands for “Splat Zip”, and SPZ was built for Niantic’s Scaniverse scanning app and released under the MIT licence.
An SPZ is roughly ten times smaller than the equivalent PLY while keeping the splats’ full spherical-harmonic colour.
sql: SQL began at IBM as SEQUEL, short for Structured English Query Language, and was renamed for trademark reasons.
It became an ANSI standard in 1986 and an ISO standard in 1987, then spread to every major database vendor.
sqlite: SQLite is released into the public domain, with effectively no copyright restrictions on its use.
sqlite3: Hipp built SQLite while working on US Navy software, after a server database kept failing whenever its host went down.
Every database in this format starts with the same 16-byte header - the text “SQLite format 3” followed by a null byte.
squashfs: SquashFS was created by Phillip Lougher in 2002 and merged into the mainline Linux kernel with version 2.6.29.
It compresses files, inodes and directories, supporting block sizes up to a megabyte for tighter packing.
srec: Each record starts with the letter S, a type digit, a byte count, an address, the data and a checksum.
The S1, S2 and S3 record types differ only in whether the address is two, three or four bytes wide.
srm: An SRM mirrors the battery-backed SRAM that an original cartridge used to keep saves when powered off.
Because it copies the game’s own save data, an SRM can often move between emulators just by renaming it.
state: A savestate serialises the whole machine - CPU registers, RAM and video memory - so play resumes at that exact instant.
Savestates rarely move between different emulators, since each keeps its own internal state at differing accuracy.
stix: STIX stands for Structured Threat Information Expression, originally developed by MITRE for the US Department of Homeland Security.
Governance passed to OASIS, and the version 2 generation was rebuilt around JSON paired with the TAXII transport protocol.
stm: Scream Tracker was written by Sami “Psi” Tammilehto of the Finnish demo group Future Crew.
The STM format predates S3M and supported four channels of sampled audio on MS-DOS PCs.
sub: The MicroDVD variant cues subtitles by frame number, so it can drift if the video’s frame rate differs.
A DVD-ripped SUB instead holds bitmap images of the text and pairs with an IDX file giving their timings.
swf: SWF first meant ShockWave Flash, later reframed as the backronym Small Web Format to avoid confusion with Shockwave.
At its peak, Adobe claimed Flash Player reached 99 per cent of internet-connected desktop PCs - a disputed figure, but one that made SWF among the most widely deployed formats ever.
swm: The split parts are read-only, and tools like DISM can later merge them back into a single WIM image.
A common split size is 4700 MB so each part fits on a single-layer recordable DVD.
sxc: The OpenOffice.org XML format was developed largely by Sun Microsystems and was also the native format of StarOffice 6 and 7.
It directly preceded the OpenDocument standard that OpenOffice.org 2.0 adopted in its place.
sxd: SXD was used by both OpenOffice.org Draw and StarOffice, which shared the same OpenOffice.org XML format.
Its successor under OpenDocument is the ODG drawing format.
sxw: The format was developed mainly by Sun Microsystems and shared with StarOffice 6 and 7 before OpenDocument arrived.
Its template counterpart used the .stw extension, much as ODT pairs with ODF templates today.
tab: The TAB file itself is a tiny text descriptor - the actual geometry lives in companion .map, .dat and .id files.
MapInfo was developed by MapInfo Corporation, later absorbed into Pitney Bowes Software.
tak: TAK was created by German developer Thomas Becker and began life under the working name YALAC, for Yet Another Lossless Audio Codec.
It is designed to roughly match Monkey’s Audio compression while decoding nearly as fast as FLAC.
tap: The extension recalls the punched paper tape that early numerically controlled machines read before computers were built in.
TAP files are usually produced by CAM software such as Mastercam, which turns toolpaths into machine-ready G-code.
tar: A tar archive is built from 512-byte blocks and ends with two all-zero blocks marking the end of the archive.
tbz: The bzip2 compression inside was written by Julian Seward and is built on the Burrows - Wheeler transform.
It typically squeezes 10 to 20 per cent smaller than gzip, at the cost of slower compression.
tcr: TCR was created by Barry Childress for the Psion Series 3 palmtops, using a 256-entry dictionary to shrink the text.
It was designed to beat the rival PalmDoc format on compression for memory-starved handhelds.
tcx: TCX extends the simpler GPX format, organising data as activities, laps and tracks rather than a bare list of points.
It can record heart rate, cadence and calories alongside the GPS route as top-level data.
tdms: TDMS stands for Technical Data Management Streaming and organises data into a file, group and channel hierarchy.
It often sits beside a binary .tdms_index companion file that speeds up reading the data.
tei: The Text Encoding Initiative traces back to a 1987 planning meeting at Vassar College and is maintained by an international consortium.
Its guidelines were originally written in SGML before moving to XML with the P5 generation.
tex: Since version 3, TeX’s version number has gained extra digits that creep towards π, signalling how stable it has become.
Knuth designed the companion Metafont system alongside TeX to define the very shapes of its letters.
textile: Textile was created by Dean Allen and first shipped with his Textpattern publishing system.
It was one of the lightweight markup languages that influenced the later design of Markdown.
tf: TF files are written in HCL, the HashiCorp Configuration Language, and describe infrastructure declaratively rather than step by step.
HashiCorp’s 2023 switch to the source-available Business Source Licence prompted the community fork OpenTofu.
tfstate: Because state is stored as plaintext JSON, it can expose passwords and keys, so teams keep it in locked remote backends.
The file maps every resource in your configuration to the real object Terraform created in the cloud.
tfw: The world-file naming rule takes a TIFF’s extension, drops the middle letter and appends a w, giving .tfw.
Its six plain-text lines hold pixel size, any rotation and the map coordinates of the top-left pixel.
tga: The Targa format and its TARGA boards came from AT&T, whose cards were among the first to give IBM PCs true-colour graphics.
It supports an alpha channel and optional run-length encoding, which kept it popular in games and 3D rendering.
tgz: The gzip half was written by Jean-loup Gailly and Mark Adler as a patent-free replacement for the older Unix compress tool.
Gzip uses the DEFLATE algorithm, a blend of LZ77 matching and Huffman coding.
thd: Dolby TrueHD compresses audio using Meridian Lossless Packing, the same scheme once used for DVD-Audio.
On Blu-ray it serves as the carrier for Dolby Atmos, embedding the metadata that places sounds in 3D space.
thrift: Thrift was built at Facebook and open-sourced in 2007, later becoming a top-level Apache project.
From one interface definition file it generates matching client and server code in many programming languages.
thumbsdb: Thumbs.db is stored in Microsoft’s OLE Compound File format, the same container used by older Office documents.
Because cached thumbnails can linger after the originals are deleted, the file is a known source of forensic evidence.
tic: TIC-80 was created by Vadim Grigoruk and imposes deliberate retro limits - a 240x136 screen and a 16-colour palette.
Cartridges can be coded in many languages, including Lua, JavaScript, Wren and Fennel.
tmj: TMJ is the JSON serialisation of a Tiled map, an alternative to the older XML-based TMX form.
Tiled was created and is maintained by Thorbjørn Lindeijer as a free, general-purpose level editor.
tmx: Tiled and its TMX format were created by Thorbjørn Lindeijer and support orthogonal, isometric and hexagonal maps.
The same editor can also save maps as JSON with the .tmj extension.
toc: Eudora was written by Steve Dorner and named after the writer Eudora Welty, for her story “Why I Live at the P.O.”.
Each .toc file indexed a matching .mbx mailbox so Eudora could list messages without rereading the whole box.
toml: TOML is designed to map cleanly onto a hash table, and its 1.0.0 specification was finalised in 2021.
It is the native format of Rust’s Cargo manifest and of Python’s pyproject.toml.
topojson: TopoJSON stitches shapes from shared line segments called arcs, so a border between two regions is stored only once.
With delta-encoded integer coordinates, files are often around 80 per cent smaller than the equivalent GeoJSON.
torrent: A torrent file is encoded in bencode and lists tracker URLs plus a SHA-1 hash for every piece of the content.
Its info section is hashed into an infohash that uniquely identifies the download across the network.
tres: Before going open source in 2014, Godot spent years as a closed in-house engine powering work-for-hire games at studios in Argentina.
The text-based TRES format is easy to read and diff, making Godot resources friendly to version control.
trp: A TRP is essentially an MPEG transport stream, so renaming it to .ts often lets ordinary media players open it.
The streams usually carry H.264 video with Dolby Digital audio captured straight off the broadcast.
truehd: Dolby TrueHD can deliver up to 16 discrete channels at 24-bit depth and sampling rates as high as 192 kHz.
It falls back to a standard Dolby Digital track when a player cannot decode the lossless stream.
trx: TRX images begin with an “HDR0” header and a CRC check, and are flashed by firmware like DD-WRT and OpenWrt.
The format targets Broadcom routers running the CFE bootloader, which plays a role like a PC’s BIOS.
tscn: Godot was created by Juan Linietsky and Ariel Manzur and named after Samuel Beckett’s “Waiting for Godot”.
On import Godot compiles the readable TSCN into a faster binary .scn file for actual loading.
tta: True Audio is fully open source under the GPL and relies on adaptive predictive filters to compress losslessly.
It handles 8, 16 and 24-bit PCM and is built for fast, real-time encoding and decoding.
ttc: TTC was developed jointly by Apple and Microsoft so related fonts could share common glyph tables.
It was devised largely for CJK fonts, where several styles reuse the same enormous set of characters.
tz: The underlying Unix compress tool uses the LZW algorithm, whose patents once hampered its free use.
It was largely superseded by gzip, which adopted a patent-free algorithm in its place.
tzf: TZF files are exported from Trimble Perspective for use in Trimble RealWorks and Business Center.
On their own they do not record the scanner’s station position, which registration software works out afterwards.
u3d: U3D was defined by the 3D Industry Forum, a consortium including Intel, Boeing, HP, Adobe and Bentley Systems.
U3D supports progressive streaming and continuous level-of-detail, so a model can refine itself as more of the file loads.
uasset: The .uasset format belongs to Epic Games’ Unreal Engine, which Tim Sweeney first wrote largely in C++ with the UnrealScript language.
When a project is cooked, an asset is split into a .uasset header plus a .uexp data file, and sometimes a .ubulk file for heavy bulk data.
ubi: UBI stands for Unsorted Block Images, a layer that handles wear levelling and bad-block management on top of Linux’s memory technology device subsystem.
UBI sits between raw NAND flash and a filesystem such as UBIFS, presenting tidy logical volumes over messy physical erase blocks.
ubifs: UBIFS - the UBI File System - was developed by Nokia engineers with the University of Szeged in Hungary and merged into the Linux kernel in 2008.
Unlike JFFS2, UBIFS keeps its index on the flash itself, so it mounts quickly without scanning the whole device, and it supports write-back caching.
ucas: UCAS is part of Unreal Engine’s IoStore packaging, paired with a .utoc table of contents that records each asset’s id, offset and size.
IoStore was introduced to speed up loading, replacing the older .pak archive approach with a content store designed for fast, ordered streaming.
uexp: Splitting the header into .uasset and the payload into .uexp lets Unreal load all package headers first and fetch the heavy data only when needed.
The .uexp companion file must keep the same name and live in the same folder as its .uasset, as neither is usable on its own.
uf2: A UF2 file is a sequence of self-contained 512-byte blocks, each carrying up to 476 bytes of payload plus a header and a trailing magic number.
The fixed block size aligns with USB mass-storage writes, so a microcontroller never receives a half-written file when you drag one across.
uimage: A uImage is a kernel image with a 64-byte mkimage header added, recording target architecture, OS, compression method, entry point and checksums.
U-Boot’s newer Flattened Image Tree format is gradually replacing the legacy uImage, bundling kernel, device tree and ramdisk with stronger integrity checks.
umap: A .umap is an Unreal Engine level that mostly holds references to .uasset files plus lighting, spawns, volumes and landscape data for the scene.
Unreal Engine 5’s World Partition can spill a map’s actors into separate external files, letting several people edit the same level at once.
umd: A single-layer UMD held about 900 MB and a dual-layer one up to 1.8 GB, encased in a small protective cartridge unique to the PSP.
UMD movie releases dried up after 2011 and the last factory making the discs closed around 2016, ending the format.
unitypackage: Unity began in Denmark as Over the Edge Entertainment and launched its engine at Apple’s 2005 Worldwide Developers Conference as a Mac-only tool.
Inside a .unitypackage each asset is stored in a folder named after its 32-character GUID rather than its original filename, which is why ordinary archivers unpack it as gibberish.
ups: UPS was created in 2008 as a successor to the IPS patch format, removing IPS’s file-size limits and adding CRC32 checksums to verify the right ROM.
A UPS patch is reversible, so the same file can both apply a change and undo it, though the later BPS format eventually overtook it on speed.
url: A .url internet shortcut is written in the old Windows INI layout, with an [InternetShortcut] section whose URL key holds the web address.
Optional keys such as IconFile and IconIndex let a .url shortcut display a custom icon for the page it points to.
usd: USD grew out of Pixar’s in-house Presto animation system, whose layering and referencing ideas were extracted into a reusable scene format from 2012.
