A .usd layer may be stored as either text or binary, and references stay valid when you switch between the two without rewriting the scene.
usda: USDA is the human-readable text encoding of USD, handy for hand-editing or inspecting a scene, where USDC is the faster binary equivalent.
USD itself is roughly Pixar’s fourth generation of composed scene description, tracing back to the single linear program files used on the first Toy Story.
usdc: USDC is USD’s compressed binary Crate encoding, designed to open faster and use less memory than the text USDA form for geometry-heavy layers.
Because USD references do not depend on encoding, a .usdc layer can be swapped for an identical .usda without breaking the scene that points to it.
usdz: A USDZ package keeps every contained file aligned to 64-byte boundaries so the assets can be read straight from disk by memory mapping, without unpacking.
utoc: UTOC is the Unreal Engine IoStore table of contents, listing each cooked asset’s id, offset and size so the engine can locate data inside the paired .ucas store.
IoStore’s .utoc and .ucas pair was introduced to load shipped Unreal games faster than the older monolithic .pak archive.
v64: The Doctor V64 stored ROMs byte-swapped from the Nintendo 64’s native big-endian order, which is why .v64 dumps differ from the native .z64 layout.
The Doctor V64 was made by Bung Enterprises and let developers and hobbyists run N64 software from CD, an affordable alternative to Nintendo’s official kit.
vbk: A VBK sits at the head of a Veeam backup chain, with later changes saved as incremental .vib files that depend on that full backup.
Veeam compresses and deduplicates virtual-machine data before writing it into the .vbk in its own proprietary container format.
vbm: A VBM is recorded by VBA-ReRecording, a special build of VisualBoyAdvance used by tool-assisted speedrunners for frame-perfect, deterministic playback.
After its header a VBM reserves 64 bytes for the author’s name and 128 bytes for a description of the recorded run.
vbox: VirtualBox began at innotek, which Sun Microsystems bought in 2008 before Oracle acquired Sun in 2010 and rebranded it Oracle VM VirtualBox.
Oracle deliberately leaves the .vbox XML schema undocumented so it can change it freely, steering users to VBoxManage and the API instead of hand-editing.
vbproj: A .vbproj is an MSBuild project file in XML, using Project, PropertyGroup, ItemGroup and Target elements to describe how to build the application.
Developers can build a .vbproj straight from the command line by passing it to MSBuild.exe, without opening Visual Studio.
vcard: Originally proposed under the name Versitcard, the specification passed to the Internet Mail Consortium in December 1996 when the Versit Consortium disbanded.
vCard 4.0, standardised as RFC 6350 in 2011, added fields for instant messaging and social profiles to the earlier 3.0 specification.
vcf: VCF files use the vCard format from the Versit Consortium, a 1990s collaboration of Apple, AT&T, IBM and Siemens.
The widely supported vCard 3.0 was published as RFC 2426 in 1998 and added UTF-8 support, with version 4.0 following as RFC 6350.
vcs: VCS uses vCalendar 1.0, defined by the Internet Mail Consortium, which directly inspired the iCalendar standard that succeeded it.
iCalendar, the .ics successor, is now defined by RFC 5545 and supports recurring events, time zones and attendees that vCalendar lacked.
vcxproj: VCXPROJ arrived with Visual Studio 2010, when Visual C++ moved from the old VCBuild system to MSBuild, so it is far more than a renamed .vcproj.
A .vcxproj is usually accompanied by a .vcxproj.filters file that records the Solution Explorer tree and a .vcxproj.user file for per-user settings.
vdf: VDF uses Valve’s KeyValues format, a hierarchical tree of key-value pairs delimited only by quotes and braces, conceptually close to JSON or YAML.
The same KeyValues format underlies many Steam and Source-engine files, including app manifests and material definitions.
vdi: A dynamically allocated VDI starts tiny and grows as sectors are first written, whereas a fixed VDI pre-allocates its full capacity up front.
VDI is VirtualBox’s own native disk container, though the program can also read rival formats such as VMDK, VHD and HDD.
vmdk: A VMDK can consist of a small text descriptor plus one or more data extents, each markable as read-write, read-only or no-access.
VMware opened the VMDK specification with revision 5.0 in 2011, and it is one of the disk formats used inside the Open Virtualization Format.
vmem: A .vmem is a raw image of a VMware virtual machine’s RAM, captured alongside the .vmss or .vmsn snapshot files that hold its configuration.
Forensic tools such as Volatility load .vmem files to reconstruct running processes, credentials and decrypted code from a captured machine.
vmg: The vMessage format is defined in the IrDA group’s IrMC v1.1 specification and was used by Nokia, Siemens and Palm devices.
A VMG embeds the sender’s details as a vCard, alongside the date, time and text of the backed-up message.
vfd: A VFD is a raw sector-by-sector image of a floppy, typically 1.44 MB or 2.88 MB, with no extra metadata wrapping the data.
Microsoft Virtual PC, Hyper-V and WinImage use VFD images to present a virtual A: or B: drive to legacy or DOS software.
vgm: A VGM logs the exact register writes sent to sound chips such as the Sega Genesis YM2612 and SN76489, timed at 44.1 kHz for faithful replay.
The header records each chip’s clock rate, so a player can reproduce the original tuning of the hardware that made the music.
vgz: A VGZ is just a VGM register log compressed with gzip, and players are expected to handle either extension transparently.
Because VGM stores chip commands rather than sampled audio, gzip shrinks the highly repetitive register data very effectively.
vhd: VHD was created by Connectix for Virtual PC, technology Microsoft acquired in 2003 and later folded into Hyper-V.
Microsoft published the VHD specification under its Open Specification Promise in 2005, letting third-party tools read and write the format.
vhdr: The BrainVision format from Brain Products GmbH splits a recording into a .vhdr header, a .vmrk marker file and a binary .eeg data file.
The .vhdr and .vmrk text files follow the Windows INI layout, and the format is one of the EEG formats recommended by the BIDS standard.
veg: VEGAS launched in 1999 as an audio-only editor at the NAMM show, gaining video editing tools in 2000.
A .veg references the source media rather than embedding it, so the clips it points to must stay in place for the project to reopen intact.
vf: VEGAS started life as a Sonic Foundry audio tool before Sony bought it in 2003 and MAGIX took it over in 2016.
VEGAS project files reference external media rather than copying it, keeping the project itself small but dependent on the original clips.
vhdx: VHDX keeps a metadata log so an interrupted write can be replayed and the disk recovered intact after a power failure, unlike the older VHD.
VHDX is optimised for 4 KB sector drives, with large block sizes that reduce I/O overhead on modern storage.
bps: BPS - the “beat Patching System” - was created by emulator author byuu (Near) as a successor to the older IPS and UPS patch formats.
Unlike IPS, a BPS patch uses delta encoding so it can describe inserted or moved data rather than degrading to storing the whole modified file.
br: Brotli is named after “Brötli”, Swiss German for a small bread roll, echoing Google’s earlier Swiss-bread-themed Zopfli compressor.
Brotli ships a built-in static dictionary of around 13,000 common words and substrings drawn from web text, which helps it beat gzip on small files.
braw: Blackmagic RAW does part of the demosaic inside the camera, which shrinks the files and makes them far faster to decode than fully raw footage.
The codec is heavily multi-threaded and GPU-accelerated, with support for Apple Metal, CUDA and OpenCL.
brd: EAGLE stands for “Easily Applicable Graphical Layout Editor” and was originally developed by the German company CadSoft before Autodesk bought it.
Autodesk has folded EAGLE into Fusion and is phasing out the standalone product, ending decades of the .brd board format’s independent life.
bson: Unlike JSON, the fields in a BSON document keep a defined order, and nested documents and arrays carry their own length prefixes for fast skipping.
BSON adds types JSON lacks, including a 64-bit Date, a Decimal128 number, raw binary data and MongoDB’s 12-byte ObjectId.
bsp: Binary space partitioning was first brought to a commercial game by id Software’s John Carmack for Doom, letting it render levels far faster.
Because building the BSP tree is slow, it is computed once when the level is compiled, which is why classic map geometry cannot move at runtime.
bsq: BSQ is one of three companion layouts - alongside band-interleaved-by-line (BIL) and band-interleaved-by-pixel (BIP) - for multi-band imagery.
Landsat Thematic Mapper scenes were distributed in BSQ form, with each spectral band stored as a complete separate block.
bundle: AssetBundles can be built with LZMA or chunked LZ4 compression, with LZ4 letting the engine decompress only the 128 KB chunks it actually needs.
The container uses Unity’s “UnityFS” layout, an uncompressed header followed by an optionally compressed body of virtual asset files.
bw: Every SGI image starts with the magic number 474, and sibling extensions signal the channels: .rgb for colour, .rgba with alpha and .bw for plain greyscale.
bw64: BW64 sidesteps the 4 GB limit of classic WAV by adding a “ds64” chunk that records the data size as a 64-bit value.
Standardised by the ITU as Recommendation BS.2088, BW64 was designed to carry the XML-based Audio Definition Model for object-based audio.
bwf: BWF was first specified by the European Broadcasting Union as standard EBU Tech 3285, extending Microsoft’s WAV format.
Its production data lives in an added “bext” (Broadcast Audio Extension) chunk carrying timecode, origin and a SMPTE identifier.
bz2: bzip2 was written by Julian Seward and compresses using the Burrows-Wheeler block-sorting transform followed by move-to-front and Huffman coding.
It works on blocks of between 100 and 900 kB, and is most often seen wrapping a tar archive as a .tar.bz2 file.
c4d: Cinema 4D began life on the Commodore Amiga, growing out of an award-winning ray-tracer called FastRay written by brothers Christian and Philip Losch.
It is made by the German company Maxon, which was founded years before the software gained its Cinema 4D name.
cab: Microsoft’s Cabinet files were originally called “Diamond” files and built with a tool named diamond.exe.
CAB archives can compress with MSZIP (a DEFLATE variant), the higher-ratio LZX, or the older licensed Quantum method.
caf: Core Audio Format was introduced by Apple in Mac OS X Tiger and uses 64-bit offsets throughout, so a single file can in theory exceed nine exabytes.
A CAF file is built as a sequence of variable-length chunks in big-endian order, with a header that names the format and its version.
capnproto: Cap’n Proto was created by Kenton Varda, who had also been the main author of Google’s Protocol Buffers version 2.
Its core idea is that data has the same layout in memory and on the wire, so a message can be used straight away with no parsing step.
catpart: CATIA stands for “Computer Aided Three-dimensional Interactive Application” and began as an in-house tool at French aircraft maker Avions Marcel Dassault.
A CATPart holds a single component; CATIA keeps multi-part assemblies in a separate CATProduct file that references them.
catproduct: CATProduct files were originally driven by software Dassault built to design the Mirage fighter jet before spinning it out as Dassault Systèmes.
A CATProduct does not contain geometry itself; it links to the individual CATPart files that make up the assembly.
cb7: The comic-archive naming convention encodes the underlying archive in its last letter, so CB7 is simply a 7-Zip archive of page images.
CB7 is rare in practice, with most digital-comic collections settling on the ZIP-based CBZ instead.
cba: The final letter of a comic archive names its container, so CBA is built on the proprietary ACE compression format.
ACE support was dropped by WinRAR after serious flaws were found in its unace library, leaving the CBA format effectively orphaned.
cbor: CBOR was authored by Carsten Bormann and Paul Hoffman, with a major design goal of fitting tiny Internet-of-Things devices.
Its data model is an extended superset of JSON’s, adding native binary strings and tagged values, and it underpins COSE and WebAuthn.
cbr: A comic archive’s last letter names its container, so CBR wraps page images in a RAR archive rather than a ZIP.
Because RAR is proprietary, CBR readers need RAR-aware code to open these comics, unlike the simpler ZIP-based CBZ.
cbt: The trailing letter of a comic archive names its format, so CBT stores comic pages in an uncompressed TAR archive.
TAR keeps no central index, so a CBT reader has to walk through the whole file to list every page.
cbz: The comic-archive convention puts the container type in the last letter, and the ZIP-based CBZ is by far the most widely used.
ccd: CloneCD splits a disc into three files: the data goes in an .img, the subchannel data in a .sub, and the control description in the .ccd.
The .sub subchannel file is what lets CloneCD reproduce copy-protected discs that older imaging tools could not.
cdb: An ANSYS CDB is produced by the CDWRITE command and stores the model as a stream of Mechanical APDL input commands.
It captures node and element mesh data so the model can be re-read with CDREAD, even on a different machine.
cdf: NetCDF was created at Unidata, part of the University Corporation for Atmospheric Research, and is funded by the US National Science Foundation.
It is a self-describing format whose header records the layout of its data arrays, so files move freely between different machine architectures.
cdi: The CDI image became the standard for Dreamcast backups because the console’s self-booting discs use multiple sessions that a plain ISO cannot represent.
It is the native image format of Padus DiscJuggler, a professional CD and DVD recording program.
cdp: In its other life a .cdp file is a CDP4 model from the COMET tool, following the ECSS-E-TM-10-25 European space-engineering data-exchange standard the ESA helped define.
Criterium DecisionPlus presents its results with rankings, trade-off views and sensitivity analysis, so a recommendation can be tested under different assumptions.
cdx: CDX is the tagged binary native format of ChemDraw, the molecule-drawing program first built in the 1980s and now owned by Revvity.
Because it is in the public domain, CDX was adopted by the US Patent Office as its standard format for chemical structures.
cdxml: CDXML is simply ChemDraw’s CDX format re-expressed in XML, so anything that fits in a CDX file can also be stored as CDXML.
ChemDraw was originally created in the mid-1980s and is today developed by Revvity, the former informatics arm of PerkinElmer.
cer: The X.509 certificate standard grew out of the ITU’s X.500 directory work, which aimed to provide a global electronic “white pages”.
A .cer file may hold the certificate as raw binary DER or as Base64-wrapped PEM text, despite sharing the same extension.
chd: CHD was written by Aaron Giles for MAME and originally stood for “Compressed Hard Drive” before being redubbed “Compressed Hunks of Data”.
It compresses the disc in independent chunks called hunks and stores SHA-1 hashes so the image’s integrity can be verified.
chm: CHM was introduced by Microsoft as the successor to the older WinHelp (.hlp) system, building help pages from a subset of HTML.
Internally the format is called ITSF, for “Info-Tech Storage Format”, and it packs its pages together using LZX compression.
cia: CIA stands for “CTR Importable Archive”, CTR being Nintendo’s internal codename for the 3DS.
A CIA bundles a certificate chain, a ticket holding the decryption key, title metadata and the encrypted content needed to install a title.
cin: Cineon was created by Kodak in the early 1990s as one of the first digital film systems, scanning frames into the computer.
Its 10-bit logarithmic encoding of film “printing density” was carried over into the SMPTE-standardised DPX format that succeeded it.
cine: The CINE format stores fully uncompressed raw frames, and its header borrows the layout of the Windows BITMAPINFOHEADER structure.
It is the native format of Vision Research’s Phantom cameras, some of which can capture over a million frames per second.
cir: SPICE’s predecessor bore the pointed name CANCER - Computer Analysis of Nonlinear Circuits, Excluding Radiation - a student-era jab at Cold War simulators for radiation-hardened electronics.
Berkeley released SPICE into the public domain, which is why nearly every commercial circuit simulator is a SPICE derivative.
vmrk: A BrainVision recording is split across three files - the .vhdr header, the .eeg raw data and this .vmrk marker file - that share one base name.
BrainVision was adopted by the Brain Imaging Data Structure (BIDS) as one of its official EEG and iEEG storage formats.
vmsd: The VMSD is the primary source the VMware Snapshot Manager reads to draw a virtual machine’s snapshot tree.
Each VMSD entry records the parent-child relationships between snapshots and the child disks belonging to each one.
vmsn: When you capture a memory snapshot, the VMSN can hold the VM’s full physical memory and CPU registers, letting you revert it while still running.
Forensic tools treat a VMSN much like a memory dump, and it even tucks away a PNG thumbnail of the VM’s screen.
vmt: A VMT is a plain-text list of key-value parameters that names a shader, such as LightmappedGeneric for walls or VertexLitGeneric for models.
VMT proxies let a material animate or react at runtime, driving effects like flickering lights and scrolling textures.
vmx: A VMX is a key-value text file holding directives such as memSize and numvcpus that set the VM’s memory and processor count.
Enabling VMware’s virtual-machine encryption scrambles the VMX too, so the normally readable settings become ciphertext.
vnt: The vNote format was defined by the Infrared Data Association in its IrMC specification, the same family that gave us vCard and vCalendar.
VNT files store their text in Quoted-Printable encoding, so spaces and line breaks show up as codes like =20 and =0D=0A in a plain editor.
voc: VOC arrived in 1989 with the very first Sound Blaster card, predating Microsoft’s WAV by several years.
A VOC file is built from typed data blocks, including dedicated silence blocks that store quiet passages compactly.
vox: MagicaVoxel’s VOX format was created by the developer ephtracy and uses a RIFF-style chunked structure.
A VOX model is capped at a 256x256x256 grid with a 256-colour palette, since voxel positions and material IDs are only 8 bits each.
vpk: A VPK is split across several files - a <name>_dir.vpk index plus numbered archive chunks each limited to about 32 MB.
VPK was first introduced in the Left 4 Dead engine branch and later backported to older Source titles.
vrm: VRM is built as a glTF 2.0 (GLB) file with extra extensions for spring-bone physics, the MToon shader and standardised facial expressions.
Because VRM fixes the rig to a metre scale and Unity-style humanoid bones, an avatar exported from one tool animates in another without rework.
vrml: VRML97, the second version, became the ISO/IEC 14772 international standard in 1997.
VRML was succeeded by X3D, an XML-based 3D format developed by the Web3D Consortium.
vrt: A VRT is read by any GDAL-based tool, letting one virtual dataset wrap files in dozens of different underlying raster formats.
The XML can apply repositioning, band selection and metadata changes on the fly, with no new pixel data written to disk.
vsd: VSD was built on Microsoft’s Compound File (OLE2) structure, organising shapes, connectors and VBA macros into internal streams and storages.
vsdx: Visio 2013 introduced VSDX to replace both the binary .vsd and the earlier XML-based .vdx drawing formats.
Visio began life at Shapeware (later Visio Corporation) and was acquired by Microsoft in 2000 for around 1.5 billion dollars.
vsix: A VSIX follows the Open Packaging Conventions, carrying a manifest and a [Content_Types].xml file alongside the extension’s binaries.
vtf: A VTF stores GPU-ready texture data using block compression such as DXT1 or DXT5, together with a chain of pre-built mipmaps.
VTF textures are compiled by Valve’s VTEX tool and paired with a .vmt material file that tells the engine how to use them.
vti: VTI is the .vti member of VTK’s XML family, holding vtkImageData - values on an evenly spaced grid such as a 3D volume.
Its payload can be stored as ASCII, Base64 or raw appended binary, with optional compression for large datasets.
vtk: VTK was created by Kitware and comes in two flavours - the simple legacy .vtk text format and a newer XML-based family.
The toolkit defines five dataset types, including image data, structured grids and unstructured grids, each with its own modern extension.
vtp: VTP is the .vtp member of VTK’s XML family, holding vtkPolyData - points joined into vertices, lines, polygons and strips.
Like its siblings it stores data in XML and is read directly by visualisation tools such as ParaView.
vtr: VTR holds vtkRectilinearGrid - an axis-aligned grid whose rows and columns can be unevenly spaced.
It is one of VTK’s modern XML formats, supporting Base64 or appended-binary data with optional compression.
vts: VTS holds vtkStructuredGrid, where the grid keeps a regular row-and-column topology but the points can sit at curved, arbitrary positions.
It belongs to VTK’s XML format family and is read straight into tools like ParaView.
vtu: VTU holds vtkUnstructuredGrid - an arbitrary mesh of cells such as tetrahedra and hexahedra used in finite-element work.
A parallel variant, .pvtu, splits one dataset across many VTU pieces for processing on multiple cores.
w3m: A W3M is really an MPQ archive carrying a 512-byte header, holding the terrain, units, triggers and scripts of a map.
Maps were scripted in Blizzard’s own JASS2 language and built in the World Editor bundled with Warcraft III.
w3x: The W3X format extended the older Reign of Chaos .w3m for the Frozen Throne expansion, sharing the same MPQ-with-header structure.
Like its predecessor it is an MPQ archive scripted in Blizzard’s JASS2 language and edited in the World Editor.
w64: Wave64 was created by Sonic Foundry, the maker of Sound Forge, before the company and product passed to Sony.
It mirrors WAV’s chunk layout but swaps the four-character chunk IDs for 128-bit GUIDs and 64-bit sizes, smashing the old 4 GB ceiling.
wab: Windows Address Book arrived with Internet Explorer 3 in 1996 and could read local contacts or query LDAP directory servers.
Windows Vista retired the WAB in favour of Windows Contacts, which kept the ability to import old .wab files.
wad: A game loads one base IWAD for its core data, then layers optional PWADs on top so a mod need only ship the lumps it changes.
Inside, a WAD is a header pointing at a directory of named lumps - the levels, sprites, sounds and textures - in one big file.
war: A WAR follows a fixed layout with a WEB-INF directory whose web.xml deployment descriptor tells the server what to run.
The structure is laid down by the Java Servlet specification, so any compliant container such as Tomcat can deploy it.
warc: WARC is the successor to the older ARC format, adding richer metadata about how each page was captured.
It was standardised as ISO 28500 and is produced by crawlers such as the Internet Archive’s Heritrix.
wbmp: WBMP was defined by the WAP Forum as a stripped-down bitmap for the Wireless Application Protocol.
Holding just one bit per pixel, it rendered as plain black-and-white (or amber and green) graphics on early monochrome handsets.
wdp: WDP began as Microsoft’s Windows Media Photo, was renamed HD Photo, then became the international standard JPEG XR.
JPEG XR was approved as ITU-T Recommendation T.832 and ISO/IEC 29199, supporting both lossy and lossless compression.
webloc: A WEBLOC is an Apple property-list file whose single url key stores the web address, opened by the Finder in your default browser.
It is the macOS counterpart to the Windows .url shortcut, created simply by dragging a page’s address out of a browser.
wem: WEM stands for Wwise Encoded Media, the per-clip format produced by Audiokinetic’s Wwise game-audio middleware.
Each WEM wraps a codec such as Vorbis, XMA or ADPCM in a custom RIFF header, and is normally bundled inside a .bnk soundbank.
wfm: A WFM is a closed, vendor-specific binary format, so a Tektronix file and a Rigol file of the same name are not interchangeable.
It is designed to be written and read quickly by the scope itself, storing the raw sample values plus capture settings.
wfp: A WFP stores only the timeline, edits, effects and settings - it references your source clips rather than embedding them.
Because the media stays external, moving a project means copying the referenced video, audio and image files alongside the WFP.
wg: WireGuard was created by Jason A. Donenfeld and uses modern cryptography including the Noise protocol, Curve25519 and ChaCha20.
Its config is a short INI-style file with an [Interface] section and one [Peer] block per remote, each peer identified by its public key.
whl: The wheel format replaced the older egg, and since 2023 the Python Package Index rejects egg uploads outright.
A wheel can be installed by simply unpacking it, with no build step, and carries its metadata in a .dist-info directory.
wim: WIM is file-based rather than sector-based, and its single-instance storage keeps just one copy of any file shared across images.
It supports the XPRESS, LZX and LZMS compression algorithms and is handled by Microsoft’s ImageX and DISM tools.
wings: Wings 3D takes its name from the winged-edge data structure it uses to track how vertices, edges and faces connect.
The modeller is written in Erlang and was inspired by the earlier Nendo and Mirai programs from Izware.
wld: A world file holds six numbers describing an affine transformation between an image’s pixels and real-world map coordinates.
The convention was introduced by Esri, and the file itself records no coordinate system - only scale, rotation and position.
wmf: A WMF file stores a list of Graphics Device Interface (GDI) drawing commands rather than pixels, so it scales without blurring.
Microsoft introduced the 32-bit Enhanced Metafile (EMF) format to succeed WMF, which was limited by its 16-bit roots.
woff: WOFF was created by Jonathan Kew of Mozilla together with Tal Leming and Erik van Blokland, wrapping the same sfnt table structure as TrueType and OpenType.
woff2: Brotli, the compression algorithm at the heart of WOFF2, was created by Google specifically to make web fonts smaller, and typically beats WOFF by about 30 per cent.
wpd: WordPerfect was created by Brigham Young University’s Alan Ashton and Bruce Bastian, whose firm Satellite Software International later renamed itself WordPerfect Corporation.
WPD is a stream-based binary format, which is why WordPerfect’s famous Reveal Codes view can show every formatting code inline.
wpl: A WPL file is built on the XML-based SMIL standard and merely points at your media files, holding no actual audio or video itself.
Because WPL stores songs as absolute local file paths, moving or copying the playlist to another computer usually breaks it.
wps: Microsoft Works was sold from 1987 to 2009 and bundled a word processor, spreadsheet and database in one cheap suite.
Microsoft retired the WPS format in 2006 and replaced Works itself with the free Office Starter edition in 2010.
wpt: A WPT template holds pre-built text, layout and graphics that Microsoft Works used to spin out fresh .wps documents like résumés and newsletters.
wri: Windows Write shipped with Windows 1.0 in 1985 as the platform’s very first word processor, and its WRI files are a binary subset of Rich Text Format.
Write became OLE-capable with Windows 3.1, letting documents embed objects from other applications before WordPad took over in Windows 95.
wrl: VRML was modelled closely on the file format of Open Inventor, a 3D toolkit originally developed by Silicon Graphics.
The XML-based X3D format was created as VRML’s successor, and a WRL file is still recognised as VRML97 within it.
ws: The WonderSwan ran on a 16-bit NEC processor yet squeezed up to around 40 hours of play from a single AA battery.
Bandai sold the WonderSwan only in Japan, where it took roughly a tenth of the handheld market against Nintendo’s Game Boy.
wsc: The WonderSwan Color could show up to 241 colours on screen at once while still running from one AA battery.
Like every WonderSwan, the Color model had no internal backlight and could only be seen clearly in good ambient light.
wtv: WTV was introduced with the Windows Media Center TV Pack for Windows Vista and became standard in Windows 7.
Unlike the DVR-MS format it replaced, WTV abandoned the ASF container and could carry MPEG-2 video with Dolby Digital audio.
wv: WavPack was created by David Bryant in 1998 and was one of the earliest lossless audio codecs, predating FLAC and Apple Lossless.
Beyond lossless and hybrid modes, WavPack can handle very high-resolution audio, including DSD and sample rates far above CD quality.
x: The .x mesh format debuted with DirectX 2.0 and is template-driven, letting applications define their own data types alongside meshes and animation.
Microsoft deprecated the .x format after Direct3D 9, dropping it from Direct3D 10 and later.
x3d: X3D was published by the Web3D Consortium and standardised as ISO/IEC 19775, offering XML, classic-VRML and compressed-binary encodings of the same scene.
X3D is a direct superset of VRML, so it can still read VRML97 worlds while adding modern features.
xar: XAR was created within the OpenDarwin project and stores its table of contents as compressed XML at the very front of the file.
Putting the table of contents first lets XAR list or extract one file without scanning the whole archive.
xbm: XBM images are strictly black and white, storing one bit per pixel as a C array of hexadecimal bytes.
An XBM file can carry a hotspot coordinate, a leftover of its original use for mouse cursors in the X Window System.
xcal: xCal was defined in RFC 6321 in 2011 and is designed so calendar data can round-trip from iCalendar to XML and back without losing meaning.
Mapping iCalendar into XML lets xCal be transformed with standard tools such as XSLT.
xcard: xCard was defined in RFC 6351 in 2011 and keeps a one-to-one mapping with classic vCard so the two can be converted back and forth.
The xCard XML schema is expressed in the RELAX NG language.
xcf: XCF stands for eXperimental Computing Facility, the student computing group at UC Berkeley where GIMP was first written.
An XCF file preserves a project’s full editing state, including every layer, channel, path, selection and guide.
xci: XCI stands for NX Card Image and is a 1:1 dump of a Switch game card, padding included.
XCI contents are encrypted, so the console’s master keys are needed to read them.
xd: Adobe XD began life as Project Comet, revealed at Adobe MAX in 2015 as a response to the rising design tool Sketch.
An XD file holds both static interface designs and the click-through interactions used to prototype an app.
xdelta: xdelta was created by Joshua MacDonald and uses an algorithm inspired by rsync to find the differences between two files.
Because an xdelta patch stores only what changed, it can be a tiny fraction of the size of the file it updates.
xln: An XLN drill file follows the Excellon format, the de facto standard that drives the CNC machines drilling holes in circuit boards.
The .xln extension is most associated with the EAGLE PCB design software.
xls: XLS uses Microsoft’s BIFF (Binary Interchange File Format) and is capped at 65,536 rows per sheet because Excel addressed rows with 16 bits.
xm: XM stands for extended module and was created by the demogroup Triton for their tracker FastTracker 2.
XM raised the channel count from the older MOD format’s four to as many as 32, and added 16-bit samples and volume envelopes.
xmp: Adobe based XMP on the W3C’s Resource Description Framework, serialised as XML, so metadata can travel embedded inside the file.
XMP was standardised by ISO as ISO 16684, cementing it as an open metadata format.
xpi: XPI is pronounced “zippy”, and modern add-ons carry a manifest.json after Mozilla switched Firefox to the WebExtensions system in 2017.
xpm: XPM was created in 1989 at the Bull Research Center in France and, unlike monochrome XBM, supports colour and transparent pixels.
Colours in an XPM image can be given as X11 colour names rather than just hex codes.
xps: XPS is built on XAML and was adopted as an international standard, ECMA-388, in 2009.
Windows 8 switched to the standardised OpenXPS variant, which uses the .oxps extension instead.
xsf: XSF stands for XCrySDen Structure File and was created by Anton Kokalj for his crystal and molecular visualisation tool.
Beyond atom positions, an XSF file can store scalar fields such as charge density and electrostatic potential for visualisation.
xspf: XSPF, pronounced “spiff”, is sponsored by the Xiph.Org Foundation, the group behind Ogg and Vorbis.
The XSPF specification reached version 1 in early 2005 after an open working group refined it.
xyz: The XYZ format originated in the XMol visualisation software at the University of Minnesota.
An XYZ file starts with a line giving the number of atoms, then a free-text comment line, before listing each atom’s element and coordinates.
xz: XZ is produced by the Tukaani Project and is the successor to the earlier LZMA Utils tools.
Inside the .xz container the data is compressed with LZMA2, typically yielding files about 30 per cent smaller than gzip.
x_b: The .x_b extension marks the binary form of a Parasolid transmit file, more compact than its text twin .x_t.
Binary Parasolid files also appear under the longer extension .xmt_bin, just as the text form is sometimes written .xmt_txt.
x_t: The “t” in .x_t stands for text, the human-readable form of a Parasolid transmit file, paired with the binary .x_b.
Parasolid traces back to the ROMULUS kernel built by Shape Data in Cambridge, England, before passing to Unigraphics and then Siemens.
y4m: A Y4M file is structured as a short ASCII stream header followed by frames, each tagged with the literal word FRAME.
Y4M was devised by the mjpegtools project to pipe raw YCbCr video between encoding tools.
yaffs2: YAFFS stands for Yet Another Flash File System and was written by Charles Manning for the firm Aleph One.
YAFFS2 is a true log-structured filesystem, tagging each block with an increasing sequence number to suit modern write-once NAND flash.
yar: YARA was created by Victor Alvarez and is now maintained by VirusTotal, which bills it as “the pattern matching swiss knife”.
A YARA rule combines text or binary strings with boolean logic to fingerprint whole families of malware rather than single samples.
yara: YARA was created by Victor Alvarez of VirusTotal, the Google-owned malware-scanning service, to classify samples by binary or text patterns rather than exact hashes.
The name is a self-referential joke, expanded variously as “Yet Another Recursive Acronym” or “Yet Another Ridiculous Acronym”.
ym: YM was devised by Arnaud Carré (“Leonard”) for his ST-Sound emulator, and the file is really a register dump of the chip replayed cycle by cycle.
The same YM2149 sound chip powered the Amstrad CPC, the ZX Spectrum 128 and MSX machines, so YM can capture tunes from across the 8-bit and 16-bit era.
yuv: The YUV colour model comes from analogue PAL television, splitting video into a brightness (luma) channel and two colour-difference (chroma) channels.
Raw YUV files often use 4:2:0 subsampling, storing colour at half resolution in each direction because the human eye is far less sensitive to colour detail than to brightness.
yy: A .yy file is written by GameMaker Studio 2 from YoYo Games, with one such file describing each sprite, object, room or other asset in a game.
Because the format is text-based and human-readable, .yy files sit comfortably under version control alongside a game’s source.
yyp: The .yyp sits at the root of a GameMaker project and lists every resource plus project-wide settings, acting as the index the IDE loads first.
It is accompanied by a separate “.resource_order” file that records the custom ordering of assets shown in the editor’s resource tree.
z: The .Z format uses the LZW algorithm, which was independently implemented by Spencer Thomas at the University of Utah without realising a patent was pending on it.
Royalty demands over the underlying LZW patent, later held by Unisys, helped drive users towards the patent-free gzip and its .gz files.
z64: Despite naming the format, .z64 actually stores the ROM in the Nintendo 64’s native big-endian byte order, while rival dumps from the Doctor V64 came out byte-swapped.
The extension comes from the Z64 backup unit by Interesting Devices, one of several copier cartridges whose differing byte orders spawned the .z64, .v64 and .n64 variants.
zae: ZAE stands for Zipped Asset Exchange and was added in COLLADA 1.5, motivated by Google to package models for its 3D Warehouse and SketchUp tool.
A ZAE archive must contain a manifest.xml whose <dae_root> element points to the main .dae document an application should load first.
zoo: The Zoo archiver was written by Rahul Dhesi in the mid-1980s and built on the LZSS compression algorithm.
Distributed as source on the comp.sources.misc Usenet group, Zoo ran across Unix, MS-DOS, AmigaOS and even VAX/VMS machines before ZIP eclipsed it.
zpr: A .zpr saves the entire ZBrush workspace - the model, materials, lighting, render settings, timeline and canvas layout - so an artist can resume exactly where they left off.
ZBrush was created by Pixologic, founded by Ofer Alon, and the program is now developed by Maxon, which acquired Pixologic in 2022.
zst: Zstandard was created by Yann Collet, who is also the author of the even faster LZ4 algorithm.
The format pairs LZ77-style matching with Finite State Entropy and Huffman coding, and can train a custom dictionary to compress many tiny files far better.
Zstandard is standardised as IETF RFC 8478, which also registers the “.zst” extension and the “zstd” HTTP content-encoding.
ztl: A .ztl stores only the active ZBrush tool and its subtools, making it the compact choice for sharing a single character, prop or sculpt.
ZBrush is built on proprietary “pixol” technology, which records colour, depth, orientation and lighting per point, and the program now belongs to Maxon after its 2022 purchase of Pixologic.
cl3: Topcon’s CL3 is an indexed point-cloud format, the processed counterpart to the raw CLR file straight off the scanner.
Autodesk ReCap can read CL3 directly, keeping each individual scan position so surveyors can register the clouds together.
clr: CLR is the raw point cloud written straight from the scanner, while Topcon’s CL3 is the indexed, processed version of the same data.
Topcon pairs scanners like the GLS-2000 with its ScanMaster software to collect and process these clouds.
class: The format begins with the magic word “CAFEBABE”, a coffee-themed in-joke chosen by James Gosling’s team that foreshadowed the Java name.
cnc: Most .cnc files contain G-code, a machine language first standardised in the United States as EIA RS-274 and later by ISO as 6983.
The numerical-control language behind these files traces back to MIT researchers automating milling machines in the late 1950s.
cnt: The extension was coined by Neuroscan’s acquisition software and stands for “continuous EEG data file”.
Neuroscan, now part of Compumedics, actually ships two CNT layouts - a block-multiplexed one for its SynAmps hardware and a fully multiplexed one for everything else.
cof: Despite sitting beside the raw photo, a COF actually stores the focus-mask data Capture One uses, not the image edits themselves.
Capture One keeps these sidecars in a hidden CaptureOne subfolder and never touches the original raw, working entirely by proxy.
conda: The .conda format replaced the older tar.bz2 packages, swapping in Zstandard compression that decompresses far faster.
It splits a package’s metadata into its own inner archive, so tools can read package info without unpacking the whole thing.
conf: There is no single .conf standard - each program defines its own syntax, from Apache directives to simple key-and-value lines.
The convention is a Unix tradition, where services from SSH to Nginx expect their settings under names like sshd_config.
contact: Microsoft introduced the .contact format in Windows Vista as a replacement for the older Windows Address Book.
Each card is a self-contained XML file, so the Contacts folder needs no central database to organise people.
cop: A COP is not an edit list at all but Capture One’s cache of preview thumbnails for fast browsing.
Capture One can bundle the COP, COF, COS and raw together into a single .eip “Enhanced Image Package” for transport.
cos: The COS is the file that actually carries every adjustment you make to a raw in Capture One, since the original is never altered.
In a session Capture One rewrites the COS with each tweak, whereas a catalog stores the same settings in its database instead.
cpg: A CPG is a tiny one-line companion to a shapefile, naming the code page so accented or non-Latin text reads correctly.
It must share the shapefile’s base name, and without it GIS tools fall back to the system’s default encoding.
cpio: The name is short for “copy in and out”, after the way the tool streams files through standard input and output.
cpio first appeared in AT&T’s PWB/UNIX in the 1970s and is the archive format the RPM package manager wraps its files in.
cpr: The CPR project format arrived with Cubase SX in 2002, replacing the earlier .all song and .arr arrangement files.
Steinberg nearly called the program Cubit, but renamed it Cubase before its Atari ST launch to avoid clashing with a database product.
cpt: CPT comes from the Generic Mapping Tools suite created by Paul Wessel and Walter Smith for scientific cartography.
A palette can be discrete, giving each band a flat colour, or continuous, blending smoothly between defined data values.
cramfs: cramfs was written by Linus Torvalds as a deliberately minimal read-only filesystem for cramming Linux onto small ROM chips.
Files are zlib-compressed one page at a time so they can still be read at random, with each file capped under 16MB.
crash: On Ubuntu these reports come from the Apport tool and use the same control-file syntax as Debian package metadata.
A background daemon called whoopsie watches the crash folder and uploads each report to Canonical’s error tracker.
crdownload: Chrome renames the file to its real name and drops the .crdownload suffix only once the download finishes successfully.
Because the data is incomplete, a .crdownload usually cannot be opened, though Chrome can often resume it from where it stopped.
crl: Each CRL is issued and signed by a certificate authority, listing serial numbers of certificates revoked before they expire.
Entries can carry a reason code such as keyCompromise or superseded, defined by the X.509 standard in RFC 5280.
crm: Cinema RAW Light debuted on the Canon EOS C200, compiling the raw frames into one .CRM movie rather than thousands of stills.
The codec trims raw video to roughly a third to a fifth of full Cinema RAW size while keeping its grading headroom.
crt: The .crt extension is the Unix and Linux convention for certificates, where Apache and Nginx typically expect them.
Whether a CRT holds text PEM or binary DER is down to its contents, not the extension - the name itself implies neither.
crx: The remaining payload sits behind a header carrying the author’s public key and a signature Chrome checks before installing.
cso: CSO, also called CISO, was built to shrink ripped PSP UMD games so more titles fit on a memory card.
Its lossless compression typically saves twenty to fifty per cent, and higher levels trade smaller files for slower decompression.
csproj: A .csproj is an MSBuild file, structured around properties, items, targets and tasks that drive the .NET build.
Modern SDK-style projects shrank the file dramatically by referencing an SDK that supplies sensible defaults automatically.
csr: A CSR follows the PKCS #10 standard and is self-signed with your private key to prove you actually hold it.
Only the public key travels inside a CSR - the matching private key never leaves your machine.
ctg: Canon cameras tuck CTG files in a hidden CANONMSC folder, one per image folder, just to track how many shots it holds.
The camera deletes a CTG automatically when its photos are erased, and the file carries no image data of its own.
cube: Because a LUT only maps colour, it carries no notion of exposure or scene - feed it a properly exposed log image and you get the intended look, but it cannot rescue a badly exposed shot the way a live grade can.
The .cube text format was popularised by Iridas and adopted by Adobe and DaVinci Resolve; red varies fastest down the list of rows, then green, then blue.
1D LUTs can only bend each channel on its own (a tone curve), so they cannot change hue or saturation - that needs a true 3D LUT, which is why most creative looks ship as 3D cubes.
cue: The CUE sheet was invented by Jeff Arnold of Goldenhawk for his CDRWIN disc-burning software.
Track positions are written as minutes, seconds and frames, with seventy-five frames packed into every second.
cur: A CUR shares its container layout with the ICO icon format, differing mainly by a type flag and the added hotspot coordinates.
Like an icon it can pack several sizes and colour depths, letting Windows pick the best cursor for the display.
dae: The .dae extension stands for “digital asset exchange”, while COLLADA itself is short for “collaborative design activity”.
Originally created by Sony Computer Entertainment, COLLADA is now an open standard managed by the Khronos Group and adopted by ISO.
dav: DAV is a proprietary, often encrypted container Dahua builds around an H.264 or H.265 stream to deter tampering with footage.
Standard players choke on it, but VLC can play a DAV by forcing its H264 demuxer in the advanced codec settings.
db: SQLite, the engine behind most .db files, was placed in the public domain by its creator D. Richard Hipp.
Hipp designed SQLite in 2000 while on a US Navy contract, aiming for a database that needs no separate server.
db3: SQLite, which .db3 files use, keeps an entire database in one cross-platform file with no server process required.
Its author dedicated the code to the public domain and even refuses patches from contributors who will not do the same.
dbf: DBF was introduced with Ashton-Tate’s dBASE II around 1980 and spawned a whole family of compatible “xBase” programs.
Its header self-documents each field’s name, type and length, which helped the format outlive dBASE itself.
dbx: Outlook Express kept one DBX per mail folder, with a master Folders.dbx indexing them all.
The format carried over into Windows Mail on Vista and Windows Live Mail before later clients dropped it.
dcm: DICOM grew out of the ACR-NEMA standard, whose first version appeared in 1985 so that scanners from rival makers could share images.
A DICOM file bundles patient details and study metadata right alongside the pixel data of the scan.
dds: DDS was introduced by Microsoft with DirectX 7 to hold textures in S3TC block compression that GPUs can decode in hardware.
A single DDS can store cube maps, volume textures and a full chain of pre-built mipmaps ready for rendering.
deb: Under the hood a .deb is an ar archive holding a version stamp plus separate tar archives for control data and the installed files.
It is designed so plain Unix tools - ar, tar and a decompressor - can unpack it on almost any system.
der: DER is defined by the ITU-T X.690 standard as a strict form of ASN.1 that allows exactly one way to encode each value.
That single-encoding rule is what makes DER suitable for digital signatures, where the bytes must be reproducible.
desc: A .desc file is itself a serialised Protobuf message - a FileDescriptorSet describing one or more compiled .proto schemas.
It is produced by protoc’s --descriptor_set_out flag and lets tools parse messages or use reflection without the source .proto.
desktop: The format is governed by freedesktop.org’s Desktop Entry Specification, shared by both the GNOME and KDE desktops.
Each entry uses a simple INI-style layout under a “[Desktop Entry]” group, listing keys such as Name, Exec and Icon.
dff: DFF (DSDIFF) was devised by Philips for archiving DSD masters and can carry lossless DST compression of its 1-bit stream.
Unlike the rival DSF format, DFF has no built-in tagging, so it cannot store album, artist or cover-art metadata.
dfu: DFU is a USB Implementers Forum device class, letting a chip be reflashed using the same USB port it normally runs on.
STMicroelectronics extended it as “DfuSe”, the format its STM32 microcontroller bootloaders accept over USB.
dicom: DICOM grew out of the earlier ACR-NEMA standard, begun in the 1980s by American radiologists and electrical manufacturers.
A DICOM file bundles the image together with the patient, study and equipment details in a structured data dictionary.
dif: A DV stream is built from fixed 80-byte DIF blocks that interleave the video, audio and metadata together.
The block structure is defined by the international IEC 61834 standard for consumer digital video.
diff: The original diff used the Hunt - McIlroy longest-common-subsequence algorithm to find the smallest set of changes.
Its modern “unified” format, the kind seen in code review and Git, shows added and removed lines with surrounding context.
dita: DITA was created at IBM to cut documentation duplication, then handed to the OASIS standards body to maintain.
Its “Darwin” name nods to evolution - topics can specialise from base types much as species inherit and adapt.
ditamap: A DITA map references topics rather than containing them, so the same topic can appear in many different deliverables.
Maps can nest other maps, letting writers build large manuals and help systems from smaller reusable pieces.
divx: DivX began as a 1990s hack of Microsoft’s MPEG-4 video codec, jokingly named “DivX ;-)” by its French author.
The smiley distinguished it from Circuit City’s unrelated “Divx” disposable-DVD rental scheme of the same era.
djvu: DjVu splits a scanned page into separate text-mask, foreground and background layers, each compressed by its own method.
Its developers later became pioneers of deep learning - DjVu co-creator Yann LeCun went on to lead AI research at Meta.
dll: Dynamic linking lets Windows update one shared DLL and fix every program that uses it, but version clashes earned the nickname “DLL hell”.
dls: DLS lets a MIDI file carry its own custom instrument samples, so playback sounds the same on any compliant synthesiser.
DLS Level 2 was adopted into the MPEG-4 standard as its method for wavetable instrument synthesis.
dlv: DLV is part of the CATIA V4 family from Dassault Systèmes, used alongside .model and .exp for multi-part assemblies.
Aerospace and automotive archives still hold millions of CATIA V4 files needing decades of long-term access.
dmg: The .dmg uses Apple’s UDIF format, which arrived with Mac OS X and replaced the older NDIF disk images of classic Mac OS.
A DMG can transparently compress and even encrypt its contents while still mounting like an ordinary disk.
doc: Word debuted in 1983 as “Multi-Tool Word” for Xenix, and the .doc extension has carried that lineage ever since.
dop: A .dop is a non-destructive sidecar - it records DxO PhotoLab’s edits beside the original raw, leaving the photo untouched.
DxO PhotoLab also writes a companion .xmp file so its metadata can be read by Lightroom and other software.
dot: DOT was Word’s template format, holding the styles, layout and boilerplate that new documents are built from.
dotm: The trailing “m” marks it macro-enabled, the distinction Word draws between a DOTM template and the macro-free DOTX.
dotx: The “x” marks it as the XML-based, macro-free template introduced with the Office 2007 file formats.
dpx: DPX typically stores each frame in 10-bit-per-channel logarithmic colour, preserving the tonal range of scanned film.
Because it holds a single frame, a film sequence is stored as a numbered run of individual DPX files.
drawio: Inside the XML, each shape and connector is an <mxCell>, a structure inherited from the older mxGraph library.
Diagram content can be deflate-compressed and Base64-encoded, or saved as plain XML for friendlier version control.
drc: Draco was built by Google’s Chrome Media team to speed up 3D graphics over the web, and is released as open source.
Khronos adopted it as an official glTF compression extension, where it can shrink geometry by around ten times.
drl: The format takes its name from Excellon Automation, whose 1970s-80s drilling machines made it the de-facto PCB standard.
It is a plain-text numerical-control file listing each tool’s diameter and the X-Y coordinate of every hole.
drp: A DRP archives a whole Resolve project for moving between machines, but stores only references, not the actual media.
Blackmagic Design gained DaVinci Resolve by buying da Vinci Systems’ assets in 2009, then released it widely for free.
drt: A DRT carries a single timeline’s cuts, effects and clip references so it can be dropped into another Resolve project.
It deliberately excludes the source footage, so a recipient sees “Media Offline” until the original files are relinked.
dsf: DSF was defined by Sony and, unlike the rival DFF, embeds ID3 tags so it can store track titles and album artwork.
It carries the 1-bit, 2.8 MHz Direct Stream Digital signal that underpins the Super Audio CD.
dsi: Only a handful of DSi-enhanced cartridges were ever sold, marked by white cases instead of the usual black DS ones.
Unlike ordinary DS games, DSi titles are region-locked by a byte protected with a hash inside the cartridge header.
dsk: The .dsk (CPCEMU) format is the de-facto image for 3-inch floppies of the Amstrad CPC, PCW and Spectrum +3.
Its extended variant can store multiple copies of a sector to reproduce the deliberate flaws of copy-protected disks.
dsm: A DSM is a plain-text log of every controller and stylus input, derived from the FCEUX emulator’s FM2 movie format.
It stores a unique identifier so a save state can be checked as belonging to the correct recorded movie.
dsv: A DSV holds the in-game battery saves that real DS cartridges would keep, written out by DeSmuME as a file on disk.
DeSmuME’s name is read as “DS emu for me”, combining the console, “emulator” and a playful “me”.
dsym: The name is short for “debug symbols”, the data that turns raw crash addresses back into function names and line numbers.
A dSYM is a bundle holding DWARF debug info, matched to its app build by a shared 128-bit UUID.
ds_store: The name stands for “Desktop Services Store”, and the file is created and maintained by the macOS Finder.
Its records sit in a B-tree managed by a “buddy allocator”; left on a web server, .DS_Store files can leak a site’s folder listing.
dt0: Level 0 has a post spacing of 30 arc-seconds, roughly a one-kilometre grid - the coarsest standard DTED resolution.
Each cell covers a one-degree square of latitude and longitude, with elevations as signed 16-bit values in metres.
dt1: Level 1 uses a 3-arc-second post spacing, around a 100-metre grid, the resolution much of NASA’s SRTM was released at.
Like all DTED, it stores elevations as signed-magnitude 16-bit integers in big-endian byte order.
dt2: Level 2 packs a 1-arc-second post spacing, about a 30-metre grid - the finest of the standard DTED resolutions.
Each one-degree cell opens with fixed UHL, DSI and ACC header records before the binary elevation data.
dted: The format is specified by the US military standard MIL-PRF-89020, with named levels for increasing detail.
Files begin with fixed UHL, DSI and accuracy header records, followed by elevations as 16-bit big-endian integers.
dta: A .dta is Stata’s proprietary binary format, designed to read identically on Windows, Mac and Unix machines.
Beyond the raw numbers it stores variable and value labels, so categories like “1 = male” carry their meaning.
dtb: The device-tree idea came from the Open Firmware used on PowerPC and SPARC machines before spreading to ARM Linux.
A DTB is compiled from a human-readable .dts source file by the device-tree compiler, dtc.
dtbo: On Android, overlays live in a dedicated dtbo partition so the bootloader can patch hardware without reflashing the base tree.
Picking overlays by board ID lets one firmware image describe several board variants from a shared base device tree.
dump: A database dump is a “logical” backup - a script of SQL statements that rebuilds the tables and data when replayed.
A core dump is the opposite kind - a snapshot of a crashed program’s memory, used to work out why it failed.
dv: DV was launched in 1995 by a consortium of camcorder makers led by Sony and Panasonic, and standardised as the IEC 61834 family.
DV compresses each frame on its own using the discrete cosine transform, while leaving the audio uncompressed.
dvi: The DVI output format was designed by David R. Fuchs in 1979 and adopted by Donald Knuth’s TeX82.
DVI is a binary list of page-placement instructions that names no specific printer, font file or display - hence “device independent”.
dvr-ms: DVR-MS wraps its recordings in Microsoft’s ASF container, with the video as MPEG-2 and the audio as MPEG-1 Layer II or Dolby Digital.
If a broadcast was flagged as copy-protected, Windows Media Center stamped the resulting DVR-MS file with digital rights management.
dwarf: DWARF’s name was chosen as a medieval-fantasy companion to ELF; its creator Brian Russell read it as “Debugging With Attributed Record Formats”.
DWARF originated at Bell Labs alongside the System V Release 4 “sdb” debugger, and most targets now default to DWARF version 5.
dwg: DWG is a proprietary binary format with no official public specification, which spurred the Open Design Alliance to reverse-engineer it from 1998.
Autodesk later added TrustedDWG technology so AutoCAD can warn when a file was last saved by non-Autodesk software.
dwt: Opening a DWT in AutoCAD spawns a fresh drawing seeded with its contents rather than editing the template itself.
AutoCAD ships ready-made DWT templates preset to drafting standards such as ISO and ANSI sheet sizes.
dx: JCAMP-DX is maintained by the Joint Committee on Atomic and Molecular Physical Data, with its first standard published for infrared spectra in 1988.
Later JCAMP-DX standards extended the format to NMR, mass spectrometry and other techniques, and it now sits with an IUPAC committee.
dxf: DXF stands for Drawing Interchange Format and debuted with AutoCAD 1.0 as a neutral mirror of the proprietary DWG.
Since AutoCAD Release 10 a binary DXF variant has existed that is roughly a quarter smaller and several times faster to read.
e57: E57 uses a hybrid design - an XML section describing the scan’s structure and sensors, plus binary blocks holding the dense point data.
The ASTM E57 committee that drafted the format drew its members from scanner makers such as Leica, FARO and Trimble.
eac3: E-AC-3 stands for Enhanced AC-3, lifting the channel limit to fifteen full-bandwidth channels at up to 6.144 Mbit/s.
Dolby Digital Plus is standardised in Annex E of ATSC A/52 and ETSI TS 102 366, and stays backward compatible with plain Dolby Digital.
ear: An EAR’s application.xml deployment descriptor lists every JAR and WAR module so an application server can deploy them together.
ec3: When Dolby Atmos is streamed by services such as Netflix or Disney+, it actually travels inside a Dolby Digital Plus stream, using a technique called Joint Object Coding.
Netflix first offered 5.1 surround sound in 2010 using Dolby Digital Plus, with the PlayStation 3 as the first device able to play it.
ecw: ECW was created by Earth Resource Mapping, whose technology passed through ERDAS and Intergraph to Hexagon’s geospatial division.
ECW relies on wavelet-transform compression to reach far higher ratios than TIFF or JPEG while staying quick to pan and zoom.
edb: An Exchange EDB is built on Microsoft’s Extensible Storage Engine, also known as JET Blue, the multi-user sibling of the Access engine.
Inside an EDB the mailbox data is laid out as a B-tree of fixed-size pages, each carrying its own checksum for integrity.
edf: EDF grew out of a collaboration begun by sleep researchers who met at the 1987 International Sleep Congress in Copenhagen.
Its samples are stored as little-endian 16-bit integers, and the 2003 EDF+ revision added annotations and discontinuous recordings.
editorconfig: EditorConfig was proposed in 2012 by Trey Hunner and consists of the file format plus a collection of editor plugins.
Its sections are filepath globs in an INI-style syntax, so one file can apply different rules to different parts of a project.
eeg: In the BrainVision format a .eeg file is the raw binary signal data, paired with a .vhdr header and a .vmrk marker file sharing its name.
The .eeg data is written little-endian without a byte-order mark, and its companion .vhdr explains how to interpret the channels.
eip: An EIP keeps the original raw photo together with manifest.xml files describing every Capture One adjustment.
Capture One only packs and unpacks EIP files inside a Session, though a Catalog can still read them.
elf: ELF was designed by Unix System Laboratories with Sun Microsystems for System V Release 4 and first appeared in Solaris 2.0.
The same ELF container is used for executables, object files, shared libraries, device drivers and core dumps.
emf: Each record in an EMF maps to a Windows GDI drawing call, and the format debuted with Windows NT 3.1.
An extension called EMF+ lets a metafile also carry the richer GDI+ drawing commands.
eml: The .eml extension was introduced by Microsoft Outlook Express, and it has since become the near-universal single-message export.
EML follows the MIME standard, which lets a single message carry attachments, rich content and non-ASCII text.
emlx: An EMLX wraps an ordinary email between a leading byte-count and a trailing XML property list of Apple Mail metadata.
That property list footer records flags such as read, replied, junk status and colour labels for the message.
emz: EMZ stands for Enhanced Metafile Zipped, simply an EMF vector image compressed to shrink it for distribution.
Microsoft Office produced EMZ files when inserting clip art into Word, PowerPoint and Visio documents.
env: The .env convention began as the Ruby “dotenv” gem by Brandon Keepers, then spread to Node.js and most other languages.
Each line is a simple KEY=value pair loaded into a process at start-up so secrets stay out of the source code.
eps: EPS was developed by Adobe, the creators of PostScript, together with Aldus, and follows Adobe’s Document Structuring Conventions.
A bounding-box comment declares the artwork’s exact rectangle, and Windows EPS files can embed a TIFF or WMF preview thumbnail.
esd: An ESD is really a Windows image using solid LZMS compression, leaving it roughly thirty per cent smaller than the equivalent WIM.
Administrators typically convert an ESD back to a WIM with the DISM tool before adding drivers or updates.
evt: The legacy EVT header carries the signature “eLfL”, and each record stores its own length at both ends for scanning either way.
Classic event logs were fixed-size circular files, so once full, new records could wrap around and overwrite the oldest entries.
evtx: EVTX stores its entries in a proprietary binary XML known as BinXML, grouped into 64 KB chunks for efficiency.
Repeated event text is held once in a per-chunk template table and merely referenced by later records, saving space.
ewf: An EWF image carries case metadata such as the examiner’s name and acquisition notes, with CRCs every 64 sectors and an MD5 of the whole drive.
EnCase 7 introduced a successor, the EWF2 EX01 format, which can store more metadata than the original.
exp: An EXP is a CATIA V4 export that can act as a container holding several models at once, alongside the .model and .dlv files.
Because CATIA V4 amassed a huge user base, its EXP format remains widely importable into other CAD systems.
exr: OpenEXR stores pixels as 16-bit “half” floats - one sign, five exponent and ten mantissa bits - for high dynamic range.
A single EXR can hold arbitrary extra channels such as depth, surface normals or motion vectors, and it is now an Academy Software Foundation project.
ext: The extended file system was written by Rémy Card in 1992 and was the first to use Linux’s Virtual File System layer.
It overcame the Minix file system’s limits, allowing partitions up to two gigabytes and filenames up to 255 characters.
ext4: ext4 introduced extents - contiguous block ranges - to cut fragmentation and speed up large files compared with ext3.
It supports volumes up to one exabyte and individual files up to sixteen terabytes, and uses delayed allocation for efficiency.
f3d: An F3D is built on Autodesk’s ShapeManager kernel and preserves the full parametric Timeline of how the design was constructed.
Each save of a cloud F3D creates a new version on Autodesk’s servers, letting you roll back the design at any time.
f3z: An F3Z bundles an assembly’s F3D along with every component, sub-assembly and reference it depends on.
Fusion 360 designs use ShapeManager, a kernel derived from ACIS, so an F3Z carries full parametric construction history.
f4m: An F4M manifest lists the codecs, resolutions and multi-bitrate streams available for a piece of HTTP Dynamic Streaming media.
HDS playback driven by the F4M pulls down small .f4f segments, each only a few seconds long.
f4v: F4V is built on the ISO base media file format, the same MPEG-4 Part 12 foundation as ordinary MP4 files.
Adobe created F4V in 2007 because the older FLV structure handled H.264 and AAC streaming poorly.
fa: FASTA was created in 1985 by William Pearson and David Lipman for their sequence-search software, and was once called the “Pearson format”.
Each record opens with a “>” description line followed by the sequence written in single-letter residue codes.
faa: The FASTA format takes its name from Pearson and Lipman’s “FAST-All” search program, reflecting its use across sequence types.
In a FASTA file proteins are spelt out in single-letter amino-acid codes beneath a “>” header line.
far: Farandole Composer was a DOS tracker, and its modules can carry up to sixteen channels of sample-based music for demos and games.
Two sibling formats, F2R (linear) and F3R (block), were alternative ways Farandole Composer could save its modules.
farbfeld: farbfeld stores every pixel as four 16-bit big-endian values for red, green, blue and alpha, always keeping an alpha channel even when unused.
Rather than compress internally, farbfeld leaves an image raw and expects you to pipe it through a general compressor such as bzip2.
fasta: FASTA grew out of FASTP and FASTN by David Lipman and William Pearson, with the name standing for “FAST-All” to cover both protein and nucleotide searches.
Each record begins with a description line marked by a greater-than sign, a convention so simple it became a cornerstone of bioinformatics.
fastq: FASTQ was devised at the Wellcome Trust Sanger Institute to bundle a sequence together with its per-base quality scores.
Each read spans four lines and encodes Phred quality values as printable ASCII characters, one per base.
fb2: FictionBook was created by Dmitry Gribov and Mikhail Matsnev as an open, royalty-free alternative to proprietary e-book formats.
FB2 emphasises semantic structure over styling, tagging authors, chapters, poems and epigraphs rather than fixing their appearance.
fb3: FB3 was developed by the Russian retailer LitRes as a layout-capable successor to FictionBook 2.
Its packaging follows the ECMA-376 Open Packaging conventions, and unlike FB2 it no longer embeds images as Base64 text blocks.
fbs: FlatBuffers was written by Wouter van Oortmerssen at Google’s Fun Propulsion Labs, originally for performance-critical game code.
An .fbs schema is fed to the flatc compiler, which generates reader and writer code for languages from C++ to Rust.
fbx: FBX started as Kaydara’s replacement for the motion-capture format used by Filmbox, the app later renamed MotionBuilder.
Autodesk gained FBX through its 2006 purchase of Alias, which had itself acquired Kaydara two years earlier.
fd: An FD image is the raw contents of a board’s firmware chip, the form coreboot and EDK2 produce and flash.
feather: Feather was created by Wes McKinney and Hadley Wickham in 2016 to swap data frames quickly between Python and R.
Feather version 2 is identical to the Arrow IPC file format on disk, so the original V1 is now deprecated.
ff: An FF file stores each pixel as 16-bit red, green, blue and alpha values in big-endian order, always including alpha.
farbfeld comes from the suckless project and avoids built-in compression, expecting tools to pipe and compress it externally.
fig: Figma was built to run design tools inside the browser using WebGL, founded by Dylan Field and Evan Wallace.
A .fig file uses Figma’s “kiwi” binary serialisation, the same compact scheme that powers its real-time multiplayer editing.
fit: FIT stands for Flexible and Interoperable Data Transfer, a compact binary protocol specified by ANT.
A FIT file is built from message templates, letting one format store activities, workouts, courses and user profiles alike.
fits: The FITS header is made of 80-column “card images”, a layout inherited from the era of punch cards.
Its guiding rule, “once FITS, always FITS”, requires every change to the standard to stay backward compatible.
fla: An FLA holds the editable project, including timelines, vector art and ActionScript, that compiles down to a SWF for playback.
The format began as FutureSplash before Macromedia bought it in 1996 and rebranded it Flash.
flatpak: Flatpak began as xdg-app, created by Alexander Larsson at Red Hat before its 2016 rename.
It splits applications from shared runtimes and uses OSTree to download and de-duplicate them across distributions.
flp: FL Studio is made by the Belgian company Image-Line, and its FruityLoops original was the work of sole developer Didier Dambrin.
An FLP saves the whole arrangement but not the audio samples it references, so projects travel light.
fm: FrameMaker was created by Frame Technology in 1986, first running on Sun Unix workstations before reaching the Mac and others.
Adobe bought Frame Technology in 1995 for around 500 million dollars, making FrameMaker an Adobe product.
fm2: An FM2 stores only the controller inputs pressed on each frame, with no video or audio, since FCEUX can recreate the run.
The format is unique to FCEUX and is not compatible with the older FCE Ultra emulator it descends from.
fna: The .fna suffix flags a FASTA file as nucleic-acid sequences, distinguishing it from the .faa used for proteins.
Like all FASTA files, each entry opens with a greater-than header line followed by the raw sequence letters.
fods: FODS is the flat, single-file form of an OpenDocument spreadsheet, storing everything as one uncompressed XML file.
Because it is plain XML rather than a zipped package, version-control tools like Git can diff its changes line by line.
fodt: FODT is the flat-XML variant of an OpenDocument text document, written as a single uncompressed file rather than a zip package.
Its readable XML makes it popular for Git tracking and automated document generation, at the cost of a larger file size.
fpf: FPF stands for FLIR Public File Format, opened up so users can analyse raw thermal data in their own software.
After its header, an FPF holds a matrix of single-precision floating-point values, one temperature per pixel.
fq: Each FASTQ entry uses four lines: an identifier, the sequence, a separator and a line of per-base quality characters.
The format originated at the Wellcome Trust Sanger Institute to keep reads and their quality scores together.
fsb: FSB stands for FMOD Sample Bank, generated by the FSBank tool to pack many game sounds into one file.
FMOD is made by Firelight Technologies, and newer FMOD Studio projects use .bank files instead of FSB.
fsproj: In an .fsproj the order of files matters: F# compiles them top to bottom and forbids forward references to code not yet seen.
F# was designed by Don Syme at Microsoft Research as a .NET member of the ML family, modelled on OCaml.
fts: A FITS header is composed of fixed 80-character “cards”, a width carried over from punch-card systems.
The format follows the motto “once FITS, always FITS”, so revisions to the standard must remain backward compatible.
gco: The “G” in G-code marks a preparatory command, the family of moves and modes the controller prepares before acting.
Alongside G-words, M-words handle miscellaneous functions such as turning a spindle, fan or coolant on and off.
gcode: G-code is standardised as EIA RS-274 and internationally as ISO 6983, with the “G” denoting its preparatory commands.
gdb: An Esri file geodatabase is a folder of binary files, the successor to the Access-based personal geodatabase.
Each table or feature class can reach a terabyte by default, far beyond what shapefiles were built to hold.
gem: A .gem is a tar archive bundling data.tar.gz, a gzipped YAML metadata file and a checksums file.
Signed gems add matching .sig signature files so the package contents can be cryptographically verified.
gen: The Mega Drive pairs a Motorola 68000 main processor with a Zilog Z80 that mainly drives the sound hardware.
Later consoles required a “SEGA” string near the start of the ROM, part of the Trademark Security System.
gg: The Game Gear reused Master System hardware, sharing its Zilog Z80 processor so it could run adapted Master System games.
Its standout feature was a backlit colour screen drawing from a palette of 4,096 colours.
gguf: GGUF stands for GGML Universal File and packs weights plus structured key-value metadata into one self-contained file.
It was designed as an extensible, backward-compatible successor to the older GGML format.
gb: The Game Boy runs a Sharp LR35902, a processor that blends features of the Intel 8080 and the Zilog Z80.
Every cartridge must hold the exact Nintendo logo bytes in its header, or the boot ROM refuses to start the game.
gba: The Game Boy Advance is driven by a 32-bit ARM7TDMI, a leap over its 8-bit predecessors.
It keeps a separate Sharp processor on board purely to stay backward compatible with Game Boy and Game Boy Color cartridges.
gbc: The Game Boy Color uses the same Sharp core as the original handheld but adds a double-speed mode running at about 8 MHz.
It stays fully backward compatible, playing the entire original Game Boy library.
gbl: In the Protel naming scheme a .gbl is the Gerber Bottom copper Layer, the “G”, “B” and “L” spelling out its role.
That Protel extension convention became the de facto standard most board fabricators expect.
gbr: The format is named after Joseph Gerber, whose numerically controlled photoplotter exposed PCB artwork through shaped apertures.
The format is now maintained by the Belgian company Ucamco, which bought Gerber’s PCB division.
gbs: A GBS file carries the actual Game Boy sound-driver code ripped from a ROM, so a player must emulate the hardware to hear it.
It was created to replace the earlier GBR format, whose header lacked enough detail for faithful playback.
gh: Grasshopper was created by David Rutten at Robert McNeel & Associates and first shipped in 2007 under the name “Explicit History”.
The .gh file is the binary form of a Grasshopper definition, fully interchangeable with the XML-based .ghx that holds the very same node graph.
ghx: GHX is the human-readable XML twin of the binary .gh file, both deserialising to an identical Grasshopper definition inside Rhino.
A huge plug-in ecosystem hangs off Grasshopper definitions, from Kangaroo for live physics simulation to Ladybug for environmental analysis.
gifv: Imgur introduced GIFV in October 2014, automatically transcoding uploaded animated GIFs to H.264 MP4 or WebM to slash their file size.
Despite the name, a GIFV link serves a looping silent HTML5 video tag, not the decades-old GIF bitmap format it imitates.
gig: The GIG format debuted with Nemesys GigaSampler, whose patented “Endless Wave” technology streamed samples straight from disk instead of loading them into RAM.
Tascam acquired GigaStudio and the GIG format in 2001, and after Tascam stopped development the technology passed to Garritan in 2009.
glb: GLB is the binary container for glTF 2.0, packing a JSON description chunk and a raw binary buffer chunk into one self-contained file.
glTF 2.0, and with it the GLB packaging, was adopted as international standard ISO/IEC 12113 in 2022.
gltf: glTF stands for GL Transmission Format, designed by the Khronos Group as an efficient way to deliver 3D scenes to graphics applications and the web.
glTF 2.0 became international standard ISO/IEC 12113 in 2022, and the format is widely nicknamed “the JPEG of 3D”.
gml: GML was also adopted by ISO as standard 19136, the result of collaboration between the Open Geospatial Consortium and ISO’s geographic-information committee.
GML can describe not just points and lines but coverages, coordinate reference systems and rich geographic feature models.
gmx: GameMaker stored each resource in its own XML file with a doubled extension, such as .object.gmx or .sprite.gmx, inside a GameMaker: Studio 1 project.
YoYo Games retired the GMX format in GameMaker Studio 2, replacing it with the JSON-based YYP project structure.
godot: The project.godot file is the INI-style entry point the engine scans to recognise a folder as a Godot project.
It replaced the engine.cfg file used up to Godot 2, taking the new name when Godot 3 arrived.
gpg: GnuPG was started in 1997 by Werner Koch after Richard Stallman called for a free replacement for the export-restricted PGP, with its first release in 1999.
A GPG file is protected with hybrid encryption, combining fast symmetric ciphers for the data with public-key cryptography for the keys.
gpkg: GeoPackage was published as an Open Geospatial Consortium standard in 2014, developed with backing from the US military as a modern successor to the shapefile.
Because it is a single SQLite database, a GeoPackage can hold vector features, raster tiles and plain tables together and scale far past the shapefile’s 2 GB ceiling.
gpt: The GUID Partition Table was devised by Intel as part of the EFI specification and later folded into the UEFI standard.
GPT keeps both a primary and a backup copy of the partition table for resilience, and typically supports up to 128 partitions per disk.
To stop older tools from seeing a GPT disk as empty, a “protective MBR” is written in the very first sector ahead of the GPT header.
gql: GraphQL was built inside Facebook in 2012 to power its native News Feed apps and was released publicly in 2015.
Stewardship of GraphQL passed in 2018 to the GraphQL Foundation, hosted by the Linux Foundation.
