gradle: Gradle was created in 2007 by Hans Dockter to combine Ant’s flexibility with Maven’s convention-over-configuration approach.
A .gradle build script can be written in a Groovy or a Kotlin domain-specific language, and the tool’s incremental builds skip work that is already up to date.
graphql: GraphQL was developed inside Facebook to build its native News Feed and shipped into production in 2012, three years before its public release.
Governance of GraphQL moved in 2018 to the GraphQL Foundation under the Linux Foundation, with members including GitHub, Shopify and Twitter.
grb: GRIB stands for “gridded binary”, the format meteorological centres use to pack numerical weather-prediction output compactly.
The original GRIB edition was created by the World Meteorological Organization in the 1980s and is now largely superseded by GRIB2.
grd: A GRD file opens with the signature “8BGR” and stores its gradients as big-endian binary data for Adobe Photoshop.
Each gradient stop can record colour in several models, including RGB, HSV, CMYK, Lab and grayscale.
grib: GRIB was created by the World Meteorological Organization’s Commission for Basic Systems, with the first edition introduced in the mid-1980s.
GRIB2, the second-edition format, became the WMO’s official exchange standard and added template-based descriptions and better compression.
grib2: GRIB2 specifies coordinates in micro-degrees rather than the milli-degrees of GRIB1, giving far finer geographic precision.
GRIB2 became the World Meteorological Organization’s official format, and a successor GRIB3 edition is now under discussion.
gsm: The GSM audio codec is GSM 06.10 Full Rate, standardised by the European Telecommunications Standards Institute for early digital mobile calls.
It uses Regular Pulse Excitation with Long Term Prediction to squeeze 20-millisecond speech frames down to about 13 kbit/s.
gtl: The Gerber format is named after Joseph Gerber, whose company built the photoplotters that first turned these files into circuit-board artwork.
Extended Gerber (RS-274X), now stewarded by Ucamco, embeds all aperture definitions directly in each layer file so no separate table is needed.
gxf: GXF was developed by the Grass Valley Group and standardised by SMPTE as SMPTE 360M for professional broadcast interchange.
As a container it can carry Motion JPEG, MPEG or DV video together with audio, timecode and user metadata.
har: The HAR format grew out of a collaboration between the makers of Firebug and HttpWatch to standardise a JSON log of browser network activity.
A draft to formalise HAR through the W3C Web Performance Working Group was submitted but never ratified, so the community 1.2 specification remains the reference.
hcl: HCL originated in HashiCorp’s Terraform, with its original parser written by company co-founder Mitchell Hashimoto.
Every HCL document has an equivalent JSON form, so humans can hand-write the native syntax while machines generate the JSON variant.
hdd: By default a Parallels HDD is an “expanding” disk that only grows on the Mac as the virtual machine actually fills it, rather than reserving its full size up front.
The .hdd image normally lives tucked inside the virtual machine’s .pvm bundle on disk.
hdp: HDP began as Microsoft’s Windows Media Photo, was renamed HD Photo, and first shipped with Windows Vista.
HD Photo was later standardised as JPEG XR, published as ITU-T Recommendation T.832 in 2009.
hdr: The Radiance HDR format was created by Greg Ward as part of the Radiance lighting-simulation system at Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory.
It uses an RGBE encoding in which three colour bytes share a single exponent byte, capturing a vast brightness range in just 32 bits per pixel.
hea: A HEA file is the WFDB header that describes a recording’s channels, sampling rate and gain, paired with a separate .dat file holding the raw signal.
Header and data files share a record name, as in the MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database, where record 100 consists of 100.hea, 100.dat and 100.atr.
hex: Intel HEX was devised in 1973 for Intel’s Intellec development systems, originally to load programs from paper tape.
Each text record ends in a checksum that is the two’s complement of the sum of its bytes, guarding against corruption while flashing chips.
hgt: The HGT extension simply stands for “height”, the elevation value stored for each grid cell.
The data came from a radar flown aboard the Space Shuttle Endeavour during an 11-day mission in February 2000.
hip: Houdini is made by SideFX, founded as Side Effects Software in 1987 by Kim Davidson and Greg Hermanovic, who adapted it from their earlier PRISMS toolset.
The Houdini name itself was introduced in 1996, separating the new application from the PRISMS suite it grew out of.
hjson: Hjson was created by Christian Zangl to spare people JSON’s strict punctuation, allowing comments, unquoted keys and strings, and optional or trailing commas.
It is meant purely as a human-facing editing layer, expected to be converted to plain JSON before a program consumes it.
hwp: HWP is built on Microsoft’s Compound File Binary format, storing multiple data streams within one file much like an old-style file system.
Hangul 1.0 was released in 1989 by a Seoul National University computer club, and Hancom published the HWP binary specification publicly in 2010.
hwpx: HWPX is South Korea’s national document standard KS X 6101, based on the XML-based OWPML markup, and became the default save format for Hancom Office in 2021.
Like DOCX, an HWPX file is a ZIP package of XML parts describing the document’s content, layout and styling.
iam: An Inventor assembly does not store part geometry itself; it references separate .ipt part files and records how they are positioned and constrained together.
Inventor’s parametric models let a single change, such as a hole’s diameter, ripple automatically through the linked parts and the assembly.
ibooks: The .ibooks format was built on EPUB but extended with Apple’s interactive widgets such as galleries, quizzes and embedded media.
Apple retired iBooks Author in 2020 and folded its book-making features into the Pages app.
ical: Content lines in an iCalendar file may be at most 75 octets long - anything longer is folded onto the next line, which starts with a single space.
Beyond events, the format defines components for to-do lists (VTODO), journal entries (VJOURNAL) and reminder alarms (VALARM).
icns: The ICNS format arrived with Mac OS X in 2001, succeeding the resource-fork icons of classic Mac OS.
It later gained @2x Retina variants, and its largest icons are stored using PNG or JPEG 2000 compression rather than raw pixels.
ics: iCalendar was created in 1998 by an IETF working group, authored by Frank Dawson of Lotus and Derik Stenerson of Microsoft.
It descends from the earlier vCalendar (.vcs) format, and its current definition is RFC 5545, which superseded RFC 2445 in 2009.
idml: Unpacking an IDML file reveals a directory of XML parts compressed with ZIP, describing the document much as DOCX does for Word.
idw: An IDW drawing can only be opened in Inventor or the free Inventor View, whereas Inventor’s rival DWG drawings open in AutoCAD too.
Inventor keeps drawing data in either IDW or DWG files that look identical, but IDW files are the smaller of the two.
ifb: An IFB file uses iCalendar’s VFREEBUSY component, the same RFC 5545 standard as event files but carrying only blocks of busy and free time.
VFREEBUSY data can be a request for someone’s availability, a reply to such a request, or a simply published schedule of busy periods.
ifc: IFC was created by buildingSMART, the body once known as the International Alliance for Interoperability, and is published as ISO standard 16739.
IFC models are usually written in the STEP physical file format, the same plain-text encoding used by mechanical CAD exchange files.
ifczip: An IFCZIP normally wraps a single STEP-format IFC file, and the compression can shrink a large building model by around 80 to 90 per cent.
buildingSMART gives the IFCZIP extension its own official logo, treating the zipped model as a first-class way to exchange BIM data.
igc: IGC files must record barometric pressure altitude alongside satellite position, so a record cannot be faked simply by spoofing GNSS height.
Many IGC recorders log an Engine Noise Level value in each fix, letting officials tell whether a motor-glider ran its engine in flight.
ima: An IMA file is a raw dump of every sector on a floppy with no header or metadata, most often a 1.44 MB 3.5-inch disk.
WinImage popularised the IMA dump and its compressed cousin IMZ, letting old diskettes be archived and re-written byte for byte.
img: The .img extension is notoriously ambiguous, also used for GEM raster graphics, Garmin map data and Android system images.
Because an IMG copies a whole drive rather than one volume, it begins with the partition table and is the usual format for Raspberry Pi SD-card backups.
imy: iMelody was defined by the IrMC specification of the Infrared Data Association and adopted as the ringtone format for Enhanced Message Service.
Beyond the monophonic tune, an iMelody can drive the phone’s backlight, indicator LEDs and vibration motor.
indd: InDesign was code-named “K2” during development and nicknamed the “Quark Killer”, aimed squarely at the then-dominant QuarkXPress.
InDesign is built as a tiny core with almost every feature added as a plug-in, an object-oriented design carried over from the K2 project.
indt: InDesign templates and documents share the same internal format, the .indt extension just marking a file meant to be reused rather than edited in place.
Alongside INDD and INDT, InDesign uses IDML as an XML interchange format for moving layouts between versions or other software.
ini: The INI format was never formally standardised, so parsers vary on details like comment characters and Unicode, with Microsoft’s own files writing plain ANSI text.
inp: An INP deck uses a keyword-driven syntax in which each keyword line is followed by data lines giving nodes, elements and material tables.
Abaqus ships translators that convert its INP keyword format to and from Nastran’s bulk-data deck, letting the two solvers swap models.
insp: Despite its own extension, an INSP is essentially a JPEG, holding the still image an Insta360 camera produces.
An INSP carries the camera’s metadata so software knows how to project and stitch the dual-fisheye capture into a 360-degree photo.
insv: An INSV is really an MP4 carrying dual-fisheye footage plus gyroscope data, so renaming it to .mp4 reveals the unstitched video.
Insta360 cameras can split one recording across several INSV files, separating the front and back lenses and a low-resolution preview track.
ioc: OpenIOC describes threat indicators using Boolean AND and OR logic and defines over 500 indicator terms that can be extended as needed.
Mandiant released OpenIOC with free tools such as the IOC Editor for building indicators and a finder for scanning a single host.
ipa: Inside an IPA the app lives in a Payload folder as a .app bundle, holding the binary, an Info.plist and a _CodeSignature directory of signed checksums.
ips: IPS stands for “International Patching System”, and its patches begin with the marker “PATCH” and end with an “EOF” marker.
Because IPS uses three-byte offsets it cannot patch beyond about 16 MiB, and it carries no checksum to confirm the right file was patched.
ipt: An IPT stores not just the finished solid but the full feature history and parameters, so the model can be re-edited by changing a dimension.
Inventor part files keep precise boundary-representation geometry and can also be opened and edited in Autodesk Fusion 360.
ipynb: The name Jupyter blends Julia, Python and R, the three languages the project first set out to support.
The notebook began life inside the IPython project led by Fernando Pérez and Brian Granger before being spun out under the Jupyter name.
ism: The ISM is a server-side manifest that IIS uses to map incoming requests to media files and is never sent to the player itself.
Smooth Streaming stores its media as fragmented MP4 in .ismv and .isma files that the ISM ties together.
ismc: The ISMC is the client manifest delivered to the player, listing the available quality levels of a Smooth Streaming presentation.
By convention an ISMC client manifest shares its base name with the server-side ISM that describes the same stream.
iso: Extensions layered onto ISO 9660 add features the base lacked: Joliet for Unicode names, Rock Ridge for Unix permissions and El Torito for bootable discs.
Newer discs often use the Universal Disk Format instead, which the ISO image can also carry for DVDs and Blu-rays.
isz: ISZ is advertised as “ISO Zipped” and squeezes the image with zlib or bzip2, with optional AES encryption.
A large ISZ can be split into numbered segments, the first keeping the .isz extension and later parts using .i01, .i02 and so on.
it: Impulse Tracker was written by Jeffrey Lim in assembly language, its interface modelled on Scream Tracker 3.
The IT format added New Note Actions, virtual channels that let a note keep ringing past its slot for up to 256 voices.
itb: FIT stands for “Flattened Image Tree”, and the file is built with U-Boot’s mkimage tool from a device-tree-style source.
A single FIT image can bundle several kernel and device-tree pairs as named configurations, letting one image boot different hardware variants.
ivf: The IVF name dates back to Intel’s old Indeo Video codec but the container was revived by On2 and Google as a bare test wrapper for VP8.
An IVF starts with a 32-byte file header and gives each frame a small 12-byte header, deliberately omitting audio and rich metadata.
j2k: JPEG 2000 uses a wavelet transform rather than the discrete cosine transform of ordinary JPEG, offering lossless and lossy coding in one design.
Raw JPEG 2000 codestreams open with the two-byte start-of-codestream marker FF 4F, which is how decoders tell them apart from boxed JP2 files.
j64: The Jaguar’s “64-bit” claim rested on two custom 32-bit chips named Tom and Jerry working alongside a Motorola 68000 processor.
Atari pushed the figure with the tagline “Do the math!”, a boast that rival magazines openly mocked.
jar: A JAR carries a MANIFEST.MF file in its META-INF folder that can name the main class to run and list classpath dependencies.
Signing a JAR does not sign the archive itself but records and signs a checksum for every file it contains.
jats: JATS is the NISO Z39.96 standard and grew out of a document type definition created by the US National Library of Medicine for PubMed Central.
The standard actually offers three related tag sets, tuned separately for authoring, publishing and long-term archiving of articles.
jcal: jCal is defined by RFC 7265 and was produced by the IETF working group that also created the JSON form of vCard.
jCal is designed to round-trip, so converting an iCalendar file to jCal and back preserves every component and property.
jcard: jCard is specified in RFC 7095 by the same IETF working group behind the JSON calendar format jCal.
jCard was created so JavaScript widgets and web apps can handle contact data without parsing the older vCard text syntax.
jceks: The name stands for Java Cryptography Extension KeyStore, and unlike plain JKS it can store symmetric secret keys such as AES and DES.
JCEKS protects entries with PBEWithMD5AndTripleDES, algorithms now considered weak and best replaced by the modern PKCS#12 format.
jdx: JCAMP stands for the Joint Committee on Atomic and Molecular Physical Data, with IUPAC taking over the standard in 1995.
Because the data is plain ASCII, a JCAMP-DX spectrum can survive being printed and later scanned back in by an optical reader.
jffs2: The original JFFS was designed by Sweden’s Axis Communications, with JFFS2 then developed at Red Hat by David Woodhouse.
As a log-structured filesystem JFFS2 only appends new nodes and reclaims space with garbage collection, never overwriting data in place.
jgw: The odd name follows the world-file rule of taking the first and last letters of the image extension plus “w”, so jpg becomes jgw.
A JGW holds six numbers giving pixel size, rotation and the map coordinate of the top-left pixel, with the y-scale usually negative.
jks: JKS protects each private key with a home-grown cipher built from SHA-1 and the password rather than a recognised encryption standard.
Unlike its JCEKS relative, JKS can only hold asymmetric private keys and certificates, never symmetric secret keys.
jnlp: JNLP stands for the Java Network Launch Protocol, the XML file that told Java Web Start how to fetch and run an application.
Oracle deprecated Java Web Start in Java 9 and removed it in Java 11, leaving community projects like IcedTea-Web and OpenWebStart to carry it on.
job: A .job file stored a single Task Scheduler entry in the older Windows scheduler before newer versions moved to XML task definitions.
These task files lived in the Windows Tasks folder, each one holding the trigger and program for a scheduled action.
jp2: JP2 is the boxed file format of JPEG 2000, wrapping the wavelet codestream together with metadata such as colour and resolution.
JPEG 2000 found a lasting home in digital cinema, where the DCI standard adopted it for distributing feature films.
jpc: JPC has no officially standardised extension - .j2k and .j2c name the same raw codestream and are used interchangeably.
A JPC codestream is meant to be embedded inside a JP2 wrapper, but it can also be stored on its own as a bare file.
jpf: “jpf” is the file suffix the JPEG 2000 Part 2 standard actually recommends for these extended JPX images, though .jpx is also seen.
JPX adds animation and the compositing of several codestreams into a single image, capabilities the basic JP2 format lacks.
jpx: JPX is defined by JPEG 2000 Part 2, the extensions standard ISO/IEC 15444-2, built on top of the Part 1 core.
A JPX file is built from nested tagged “boxes”, the same container approach used by QuickTime and MP4.
json5: JSON5 was created by Aseem Kishore and is nicknamed “JSON for Humans”, aimed squarely at hand-edited configuration files.
JSON5 allows comments, single-quoted strings, hexadecimal numbers and trailing commas, none of which strict JSON permits.
jsonc: JSONC was informally introduced by Microsoft for Visual Studio Code config files such as settings.json, tasks.json and launch.json.
Microsoft ships a small jsonc-parser library so tools can read JSONC, since the plain JSON.parse chokes on its comments.
jsonl: JSON Lines puts one complete JSON value per line and, unlike a JSON array, uses no commas or brackets between records.
JSON Lines was once a separate format from NDJSON, but the two communities reconciled them into a single shared convention.
jt: JT stands for Jupiter Tessellation, and the format was originally designed by Engineering Animation and Hewlett-Packard as the Direct Model toolkit.
JT passed through UGS, which named it their master 3D format, before Siemens acquired UGS and made JT its interoperability standard.
jwt: A JWT has three Base64url-encoded parts - header, payload and signature - joined by full stops, so it travels safely in URLs and headers.
The signature lets a server verify a JWT has not been tampered with, without having to store any session state of its own.
jxr: JPEG XR began life as Microsoft’s HD Photo, which was itself first announced as Windows Media Photo.
A JPEG XR image can be split into tiles that decode independently, so regions can be cropped or rotated without unpacking the whole file.
kdb: KDB is the database format of KeePass, the open-source password manager created by Dominik Reichl.
KDB was the format of KeePass 1.x and was superseded by KDBX when the manager moved to its version 2 line.
kdbx: Inside a KDBX file the entries are held as XML, optionally GZip-compressed, before the whole payload is encrypted.
KDBX can encrypt that payload with AES-256, ChaCha20 or Twofish, the cipher being chosen when the database is created.
key: A .key file is often PEM text - Base64 between “BEGIN” and “END” lines - which is just a wrapper around the binary DER key.
The same .key extension is reused for many key types, from TLS server keys to PKCS#8 private keys, so contents vary widely.
keychain: Apple’s keychain idea dates back to the PowerTalk mail engine in the early System 7 era, long before Mac OS X revived it.
Each macOS user has a default “login” keychain that unlocks automatically with their account password.
keynote: Keynote was first sold as a standalone application before Apple folded it into the iWork suite alongside Pages and Numbers.
A Keynote document is really a bundle - a folder of XML and media that the Finder presents as a single file.
keystore: The .keystore extension usually holds a Java KeyStore (JKS), a proprietary Sun Microsystems format that was Java’s default for years.
From JDK 9 onward the default keystore type switched to the standardised PKCS12, which offers stronger algorithms than JKS.
kfx: The X in KFX stands for ten - it is Kindle Format 10, the successor to the KF8 format used by AZW3 files.
KFX added Amazon’s “enhanced typesetting” engine, bringing hyphenation, better word spacing and the Bookerly font to Kindle books.
kicad_pcb: A .kicad_pcb file is written in S-expressions, the nested parenthesised syntax borrowed from Lisp, and is plain readable ASCII.
The S-expression board format arrived in KiCad 4, replacing the older .brd layout files with a more maintainable structure.
kmz: KML takes its name from Keyhole, Inc, the company behind the Earth viewer that Google bought in 2004 and renamed Google Earth.
kra: Krita was originally called KImageShop, then Krayon, before trademark trouble led to the name Krita in 2002.
Inside a KRA the layers are stored as XML and PNG data, with a “mimetype” entry reading “application/x-krita”.
ksplat: KSplat is the native format of the Three.js GaussianSplats3D viewer, a trimmed-down and compressed rework of the original .ply splat files.
Because KSplat already matches the renderer’s internal layout, scenes saved in it load faster than equivalent .ply or .splat files.
ktx: The original KTX specification came from the Khronos Group’s OpenGL ES working groups as a container for GPU-ready textures.
A single KTX file can hold anything from a plain 2D image to a full cubemap array, complete with all its mipmap levels.
ktx2: KTX2 can carry Basis Universal textures, a technology from Binomial that transcodes at load time to whatever format the GPU supports.
Khronos defined the KHR_texture_basisu extension so glTF 3D assets can embed KTX2 textures for compact, portable delivery.
las: LAS is a binary format from the American Society for Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing, with a public header, variable-length records and point data.
LAS stores each point’s coordinates as 32-bit integers, applying a scale and offset from the header to recover the real positions.
latex: LaTeX is a set of macros that Leslie Lamport wrote on top of Donald Knuth’s TeX typesetting engine to make it easier to use.
LaTeX is conventionally pronounced “lay-tech” or “lah-tech”, since the final letter echoes the Greek chi rather than an English x.
laz: LAZ files are produced by LASzip, the open-source compressor written by lidar pioneer Martin Isenburg.
LASzip can shrink a LAS file to roughly 7 to 20 per cent of its size while preserving every bit of the original data.
ldi: LDI holds the same LDAP Data Interchange Format as the more common .ldif, just under a shortened three-letter extension.
Like LDIF, an LDI file represents directory entries, or change requests, as plain-text records separated by blank lines.
ldif: LDIF was devised by Tim Howes and colleagues at the University of Michigan and later formalised in IETF RFC 2849.
Beyond exporting entries, LDIF can also describe directory changes such as add, modify, delete and rename operations.
ldtk: LDtk stands for Level Designer Toolkit and was built by Sébastien Bénard, director of the game Dead Cells.
LDtk was previously known as LEd and can optionally export to the Tiled TMX format for engines that already read it.
lha: Such was the format’s popularity in Japan that Microsoft shipped an official Compressed (LZH) Folder add-on for Japanese Windows XP, and built it into the Japanese edition of Windows 7.
In 2010 the developer of UNLHA32.DLL ended development and advised users to abandon the format, after crafted archive headers proved able to slip past antivirus scanners.
lit: LIT, short for “literature”, is an extension of Microsoft’s Compiled HTML Help format with digital rights management added.
Microsoft Reader rendered LIT books using ClearType, the company’s subpixel font technology for sharper on-screen text.
lnk: A Windows shortcut quietly records forensic detail about its target, including the target’s timestamps and the volume serial number.
Despite pointing at another file, an .lnk is itself a structured binary file, not a copy or alias of the target’s contents.
lnx: The LNX format was devised by K. Wilkins for the Handy emulator, prepending a 64-byte header to the raw Lynx cartridge data.
That header opens with the characters “LYNX”, which tells an emulator how to interpret the ROM that follows.
lock: A lockfile records the full dependency tree, not just direct dependencies, so transitive packages are pinned too.
Lockfiles are normally checked into version control so every developer and build server resolves to the very same versions.
log: Web servers often write logs in the NCSA Common Log Format, one of the oldest fixed-field text log layouts.
Many system logs follow syslog, which tags each message with a severity from Emergency down to Debug.
logic: Logic descends from Notator Logic by Emagic, whose roots trace to the C-Lab Creator and Notator sequencers on the Atari ST.
The “Notator” prefix was dropped once the software was ported beyond the ageing Atari, leaving it known simply as Logic.
logicx: The LOGICX extension arrived with Logic Pro X, whose lineage runs back through Emagic’s Notator Logic on the Atari ST.
A Logic Pro X project is a bundle - a folder of audio and data that the Finder shows as one file.
lottie: Lottie is named after Charlotte “Lotte” Reiniger, a German pioneer of silhouette animation.
Lottie grew out of the Bodymovin plugin, which exports Adobe After Effects animations as the JSON that Lottie players render.
love: LÖVE is written in C++ and reaches the screen and speakers through the SDL and OpenGL libraries.
LÖVE is released under the permissive zlib licence and runs on Windows, macOS, Linux, Android and iOS.
lrcat: Lightroom edits are non-destructive: the catalogue records adjustments as text instructions rather than altering the original photos.
The catalogue keeps its image previews separately, in a companion .lrprev file holding a pyramid of scaled renderings.
lrf: LRF is part of Sony and Canon’s BBeB, short for “Broad Band eBook”, first used on the Sony Librié reader.
An LRF holds an unencrypted book, whereas the related .lrx extension carries the DRM-protected version of the same format.
wsp: Wondershare, the company behind Filmora, was founded in Shenzhen in 2003 by Tobee Wu and listed on the Shenzhen Stock Exchange in January 2018.
pack: Git shrinks a pack by storing many objects as deltas - just the difference from a similar object - so a long file history can take barely more room than a single copy.
Running git gc rewrites loose objects and older packs into one freshly, tightly compressed packfile.
A packfile ends with a 20-byte SHA-1 checksum of everything before it, so git can tell whether the bundle was corrupted in transit.
idx: The index lists every object SHA-1 in sorted order, so git finds one by binary search instead of scanning the whole pack.
A 256-entry fan-out table at the top lets git jump almost straight to the right region of the list from just the first byte of a hash.
The .idx carries no objects of its own - delete it and git rebuilds it from the .pack with git index-pack.
sqlite-wal: WAL journalling, added in SQLite 3.7.0 in 2010, is often faster than the older rollback journal because a commit simply appends to the log.
A checkpoint copies the logged pages back into the main database, after which the -wal file can be reset and reused.
Two random salt values in the header tag every frame of a log, so leftover frames from a previous run are easy to tell apart from current ones.
sqlite-shm: The -shm file is normally memory-mapped, letting several processes on one machine share a single view of the write-ahead log.
It stores its numbers in the host computer native byte order, because it is only ever used locally and never copied between machines.
It keeps two copies of its header and compares them, so a torn write during an update can be spotted and repaired.
db-wal: A .db-wal beside a .db is the clearest sign the database uses WAL journalling rather than the classic rollback journal.
Everything committed but not yet checkpointed lives in this log, so it is part of the database real state, not a disposable temp file.
Copying a .db without its -wal sidecar can drop the most recent changes, which is why backups should checkpoint first or copy both files together.
db-shm: The -shm file tracks which logged pages each reader should see, so several programs can read a busy .db at once without locking each other out.
It holds no database rows - only bookkeeping about the -wal log - so it never needs to be backed up.
If the matching -wal is gone the -shm is meaningless, and SQLite simply discards it.
lib: A .lib is one of two quite different things: a static library, whose compiled code is copied straight into your program at build time, or an import library - a slim table of stubs that simply tells the linker which functions live inside a matching .dll at run time.

Every member inside a .lib is a COFF object file, and its first members are a symbol index the linker scans to find which object defines a given function - the same reason renaming a .lib to .a lets ordinary archive tools list what is inside.
Because a DLL import library stores only stubs rather than real code it is often tiny - the WebView2 import library is under 3 KB - whereas a static library can run to hundreds of megabytes.
mm: Cross-platform apps like OrcaSlicer use .mm files only where they must touch macOS APIs - Retina handling, native dialogs - while the rest of the code stays plain C++.
Rename a .mm file to .cpp and its Objective-C parts stop compiling: the double m is the only thing that flips the compiler into Objective-C++ mode.
hmm: Keeping Objective-C++ declarations in a .hmm rather than an ordinary .h stops a plain C++ file from accidentally pulling in Objective-C syntax its compiler cannot parse.
A .hmm header usually wraps Apple-only types behind a plain C++ interface, so most of the codebase can use macOS features without ever seeing Objective-C.
fs: GLSL, the OpenGL Shading Language these files are written in, has C-like syntax and arrived with OpenGL 2.0 in 2004.
The .fs / .vs pairing is just one naming convention - the same code is also seen as .frag / .vert or bundled inside a single .glsl file.
vs: Vertex shaders handle the geometry maths - rotation, projection, skinning - that used to be fixed-function hardware before programmable GPUs arrived.
A .vs file is plain text, so you can open and tweak the shader source in any editor and the GPU recompiles it when the program loads.
gs: Geometry shaders sit between the vertex and fragment stages and are used sparingly, because they can be slower than alternatives such as instancing.
Because .gs is also used for Google Apps Script, the file contents - GLSL versus JavaScript - are what really tell the two apart.
jam: A Jamfile lists targets and dependencies declaratively and lets b2 work out the commands, the same idea as make but with cross-platform toolchain handling built in.
OrcaSlicer pulls in Boost, which is why its dependency tree carries .jam files such as common.jam.
build: Meson generates files for a backend - usually Ninja - which does the actual fast, parallel compilation.
The name meson.build is fixed; the same .build suffix is also used by other tools, so the filename is the real clue to which build system it belongs to.
skip: Because it is just plain text, a .skip file often holds a short reason or a reference explaining why the test is disabled.
.skip is a project convention rather than a registered format, so its exact meaning depends on the test framework that reads it.
man: A .man file is plain text marked up with roff requests like .TH and .SH, which the formatter turns into the neatly laid-out page you see in a terminal.
Manual pages are grouped into numbered sections - 1 for commands, 2 for system calls, 3 for library functions - which is why you see references like printf(3).
less: Less can run in the browser through a JavaScript compiler, but in production it is almost always pre-compiled to plain CSS first.
Its syntax is a superset of CSS, so any valid CSS file is also a valid .less file.
scss: Every valid CSS file is also valid SCSS, which made migrating existing stylesheets to Sass painless.
SCSS is compiled by tools such as Dart Sass into the .css that browsers actually load.
svelte: A .svelte file holds HTML-like markup, a script block and a style block whose CSS is automatically scoped to that one component.
Because the work happens at compile time, Svelte apps tend to ship less JavaScript than equivalent React or Vue ones.
po: PO files are compiled into compact binary .mo files that the application actually loads at run time.
OrcaSlicer ships a .po file per language - OrcaSlicer_ca.po is Catalan - which is how its interface appears in dozens of tongues.
pot: Translators copy the .pot to a language-specific .po and fill in the blanks; tools then merge later template updates without losing existing work.
Keeping the template separate means a single .pot drives every translation, so adding a language never touches the source code.
entitlements: The file is a standard XML plist of key/value pairs, such as com.apple.security.app-sandbox set to true.
A build script may switch certain entitlements off - as OrcaSlicer’s disable_validation.entitlements suggests - to ease development or notarisation.
copying: It is plain text by convention so it is readable everywhere, and package managers often look for COPYING or LICENSE to detect a project’s licence.
A file like gmp.COPYING simply prefixes the dependency name, so several bundled libraries can keep their licences side by side.
readme: Plain README files predate today s README.md - the Markdown version that platforms like GitHub render into a formatted landing page.
A name like gmp.README belongs to a bundled dependency, keeping that library’s own notes separate from the main project’s.
lesser: It was renamed from the GNU Library GPL to the Lesser GPL in 1999 to discourage the assumption that every library should use it.
A COPYING.LESSER usually sits next to a COPYING holding the full GPL, because the LGPL is written as a set of added permissions on top of it.
